effects.description: Across-notation automatic numerical processing.
effects.outcome_quote: In line with Ganor-Stern & Tzelgov (2008), we found a CDE when the task required numerical matching. We were also able to replicate the value interference effect in the physical matching task, which "suggest[s] that automatic numerical processing was taking place" (p.436)
effects.ref_original: Ganor-Stern, D., & Tzelgov, J. (2008). Across-notation automatic numerical processing. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 34(2), 430–437. https://doi.org/10.1037/0278-7393.34.2.430
effects.ref_replication: (2015). Estimating the reproducibility of psychological science. Science, 349(6251). https://doi.org/10.1126/science.aac4716

effects.description: Effects of fluency on psychological distance and mental construal (or why New York is a large city, but New York is a civilized jungle), primed participants (conceptually fluent participants) preferred concrete descriptions of the prime later, moreso than conceptually disfluent participants
effects.outcome_quote: In contrast to Alter and Oppenheimer (2008), I did not observe a statistically significant difference between the two experimental conditions in my replication attempt.
effects.ref_original: Alter, A. L., & Oppenheimer, D. M. (2008). Effects of Fluency on Psychological Distance and Mental Construal (or Why New York Is a Large City, but New York Is a Civilized Jungle). Psychological Science, 19(2), 161–167. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02062.x
effects.ref_replication: (2015). Estimating the reproducibility of psychological science. Science, 349(6251). https://doi.org/10.1126/science.aac4716

effects.title_replication: Estimating the reproducibility of psychological science

effects.description: More than meets the eye: the role of language in binding and maintaining feature conjunctions.; table: directional language (e.g., to the left) helps children bind color and shape information to a greater extent than relational language alone ( e.g., next to)
effects.outcome_quote: The present study provides a partial replication of their findings. Specifically, while replicating the direction of the effect, we failed to replicate its size and significance even though the present study had greater power.
effects.ref_original: Dessalegn, B., & Landau, B. (2008). More Than Meets the Eye. Psychological Science, 19(2), 189–195. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02066.x

effects.outcome_quote: We did not find evidence that physical cleanliness reduced the severity of moral judgments using samples sizes that provided over .99 power to detect the original effect sizes. Our estimates of the overall effect size were much smaller than estimates from Experiment 1 (original d = 0.60, 95% CI [1.23, 0.04], N = 40; replication d = 0.01, 95% CI [0.28, 0.26], N = 208) and Experiment 2 (original d = 0.85, 95% CI [1.47, 0.22], N = 43; replication d = 0.01, 95% CI [.34, 0.36], N = 126). These findings suggest that the population effect sizes are probably substantially smaller than the original estimates.

effects.description: importance of weight on having a voice in a decision-making procedure
effects.outcome_quote: There were no main effect of weight on the importance of having a voice in a decision-making procedure, importance of a book, and importance of healthy eating. However, the present design was slightly different, as the authors included three (counterbalanced) assessments of the effects. When the authors conducted the analysis on the first measurement only in the counterbalanced design, they found the effects on the perceived importance of a book, but with a smaller effect size than the original.
effects.ref_original: Jostmann, N. B., Lakens, D., & Schubert, T. W. (2009). Weight as an Embodiment of Importance. Psychological Science, 20(9), 1169–1174. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2009.02426.x

effects.description: Having variety leads people to pour more candy, presumably because variety lowers the perceived quantity.
effects.outcome_quote: We conducted six studies with methods that were similar to those used by Redden and Hoch and did not find this effect in any of them. A meta-analysis of the four original studies and 6 replication studies (N = 1,383) revealed no evidence for the phenomenon that variety reduces perceived quantity.
effects.ref_original: Redden, J. P., & Hoch, S. J. (2009). The Presence of Variety Reduces Perceived Quantity. Journal of Consumer Research, 36(3), 406–417. https://doi.org/10.1086/598971

effects.description: The presence of variety lowers the perceived quantity
effects.outcome_quote: We conducted six studies with methods that were similar to those used by Redden and Hoch and did not find this effect in any of them. A meta-analysis of the four original studies and 6 replication studies (N = 1,383) revealed no evidence for the phenomenon that variety reduces perceived quantity.
effects.ref_original: Redden, J. P., & Hoch, S. J. (2009). The Presence of Variety Reduces Perceived Quantity. Journal of Consumer Research, 36(3), 406–417. https://doi.org/10.1086/598971

effects.description: In three experiments, we tested the claim that reliable and valid measurement in the AMP depends on awareness and intentions to rate the primes rather than the targets. In short, it does not.
effects.outcome_quote: We did not replicate the finding that standard and skip AMP effects are similar to one another. Whereas Payne et al. (2013; Experiment 3) found no difference between a skip and standard AMP, we found a difference between the two, such that scores on the standard AMP were sig- nificantly larger than those on the skip AMP (which were also in the opposite direction to what the original authors found).
effects.ref_original: Payne, B. K., Brown-Iannuzzi, J., Burkley, M., Arbuckle, N. L., Cooley, E., Cameron, C. D., & Lundberg, K. B. (2013). Intention Invention and the Affect Misattribution Procedure. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 39(3), 375–386. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146167212475225
effects.ref_replication: Hughes, S., Cummins, J., & Hussey, I. (2022). Effects on the Affect Misattribution Procedure are strongly moderated by influence awareness. Behavior Research Methods, 55(4), 1558–1586. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-022-01879-4
effects.title_original: Intention Invention and the Affect Misattribution Procedure
effects.title_replication: Effects on the Affect Misattribution Procedure are strongly moderated by influence awareness

effects.description: We investigated the hypothesis that group members would reject outgroup criticism (as compared to the same criticism from the ingroup) using costly punishment
effects.outcome_quote: Classic ISE studies were largely underpowered, used inconsequential self‐reports, and no study has observed behavioural responses of bystanders versus ingroup members. [...] a high‐powered and pre‐registered 2 (Source: same‐group vs. outgroup) × 2 (Target: ingroup vs. uninvolved outgroup) experiment revealed a classic ISE and an even larger bystander ISE in self‐reports and behaviour, favouring a norm account.
effects.ref_original: Thürmer, J. L., & McCrea, S. M. (2018). Beyond motivated reasoning: Hostile reactions to critical comments from the outgroup. Motivation Science, 4(4), 333–346. https://doi.org/10.1037/mot0000097
effects.ref_replication: Thürmer, J. L., & McCrea, S. M. (2021). Disentangling the intergroup sensitivity effect: Defending the ingroup or enforcing general norms? European Journal of Social Psychology, 51(7), 1061–1072. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ejsp.2748
effects.title_original: Beyond motivated reasoning: Hostile reactions to critical comments from the outgroup.
effects.title_replication: Disentangling the intergroup sensitivity effect: Defending the ingroup or enforcing general norms?

effects.description: For auditory flankers, correct responses are significantly faster in the perceptual-congruent condition than in the response-congruent and incongruent conditions, with no difference in response times between the latter two conditions.
effects.outcome_quote: In summary, we found significant evidence for bimodal Flanker effects, F(2, 292) = 83.94, p < .001, np2 = .37, and a significant interaction of congruency and flanker modality, F(2, 292) = 4.28, p = .015, np2 = .03.
effects.ref_original: Ulrich, R., Prislan, L., & Miller, J. (2020). A bimodal extension of the Eriksen flanker task. Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics, 83(2), 790–799. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13414-020-02150-8
effects.ref_replication: Papenmeier, F., & Rebholz, T. R. (2023, September). Bimodal Flanker: Audio vs. Visual. Retrieved from https://osf.io/8nzfm/
effects.title_original: A bimodal extension of the Eriksen flanker task
effects.title_replication: Bimodal Flanker: Audio vs. Visual

effects.description: Growth mindset is associated with the change of grades in a challenging educational transition.

effects.description: Reading the word "red" before an achievement test reduces test performance as compared to reading the word of a control color
effects.outcome_quote: Experiments 1 and 2 (Ns = 69 and 104) are direct replications of Lichtenfeld, Maier, Elliot, and Pekrun (2009). Both experiments failed to uncover a red color effect on verbal reasoning scores among high school students and undergraduates (Cohen’s d = 0.04 and -0.23).
effects.ref_original: Lichtenfeld, S., Maier, M. A., Elliot, A. J., & Pekrun, R. (2009). The semantic red effect: Processing the word red undermines intellectual performance. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 45(6), 1273–1276. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2009.06.003
effects.ref_replication: Gnambs, T., Kovacs, C., & Stiglbauer, B. (2020). Processing the Word Red and Intellectual Performance: Four Replication Attempts. Collabra: Psychology, 6(1). https://doi.org/10.1525/collabra.277
effects.title_original: The semantic red effect: Processing the word red undermines intellectual performance
effects.title_replication: Processing the Word Red and Intellectual Performance: Four Replication Attempts

effects.description: social value orientation (SLM); secure attachment (AAS); sample 2 - prosocials have a higher level of secure attachment than competitors
effects.outcome_quote: we replicated earlier findings that prosociality was associated with a greater level of secure attachment and a lower level of avoidant attachment. Moreover, prosociality was positively related to the number of siblings, especially sisters, as discovered by Study 3 of the original study. However, we failed to replicate the significant relation between SVO and age. Generally, we conclude that prosocial orientation is associated—even if modestly—with secure and avoidant attachment and the number of siblings, but the link with age is a question open to future investigation.
effects.ref_original: Van Lange, P. A. M., De Bruin, E. M. N., Otten, W., & Joireman, J. A. (1997). Development of prosocial, individualistic, and competitive orientations: Theory and preliminary evidence. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 73(4), 733–746. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.73.4.733
effects.ref_replication: Liu, Y., Wang, B., & Van Lange, P. A. M. (2022). The development of social value orientation: Attachment styles, number of siblings, age, and a comparison of measures. European Journal of Personality, 37(4), 402–417. https://doi.org/10.1177/08902070221094216
effects.title_original: Development of prosocial, individualistic, and competitive orientations: Theory and preliminary evidence.
effects.title_replication: The development of social value orientation: Attachment styles, number of siblings, age, and a comparison of measures
effects.journal_original: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology

effects.description: People do deny some sentences that assert that an absence caused an event. However, people sometimes agree significantly more with sentences asserting that an event e occurred because an absence a obtained than with sentences asserting that a caused the occurrence of e.
effects.outcome_quote: Our replication produced an estimation of the effect size equal to d [ 95 %CI] = 0.57 [ 0.33 , 0. 82 ], thus replicating the effect.
effects.ref_original: LIVENGOOD, J., & MACHERY, E. (2007). The Folk Probably Don’t Think What You Think They Think: Experiments on Causation by Absence. Midwest Studies in Philosophy, 31(1), 107–127. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1475-4975.2007.00150.x

effects.description: When participants understand what determinism does mean, the vast majority take it to be compatible with free will. effects.description: in order for an action to be performed intentionally it must be performed with a relevant amount of skill or control—i.e., an intentional actioncannot simply be the result of luck.

effects.outcome_quote: Our results largely confirm the evidence documented in Poole for Brazil which suggests that when workers leave multinationals and are rehired at domestic establishments, the wages of their new coworkers who have already been present in the establishment increase. However, unlike suggested in the original article there is little indication that these spillovers differ in a statistically significant way across various dimensions of heterogeneity for any of the three countries.
effects.ref_original: Poole, J. P. (2013). Knowledge Transfers from Multinational to Domestic Firms: Evidence from Worker Mobility. Review of Economics and Statistics, 95(2), 393–406. https://doi.org/10.1162/rest_a_00258
effects.ref_replication: Haller, S. A., Flaherty, E. T., & Balsvik, R. (2023). Knowledge Transfers from Multinational to Domestic Firms: Evidence from Worker Mobility. A Replication Study of Poole (Review of Economics and Statistics, 2013). Journal of Comments and Replications in Economics, 2023–2023. https://doi.org/10.18718/81781.29
effects.title_original: Knowledge Transfers from Multinational to Domestic Firms: Evidence from Worker Mobility
effects.title_replication: Knowledge Transfers from Multinational to Domestic Firms: Evidence from Worker Mobility. A Replication Study of Poole (Review of Economics and Statistics, 2013)

effects.outcome_quote: Results indicate that few school- and student-level variables relate to reading and mathematics achievement, yet no teacher-level predictors related to first grade achievement.

Teacher qualifications and early learning: Effects of certification, degree, and experience on first-grade student achievement. effects.description: drunk utilitarian(placebo control condition)
effects.outcome_quote: Despite the use of a substantially larger sample and higher doses of alcohol compared with the ones in prior studies, alcohol had no significant effect on moral judgments. The results pose a challenge to the idea that alcohol increases utilitarianism in moral judgments.
effects.ref_original: Duke, A. A., & Bègue, L. (2015). The drunk utilitarian: Blood alcohol concentration predicts utilitarian responses in moral dilemmas. Cognition, 134, 121–127. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2014.09.006
effects.ref_replication: Paruzel-Czachura, M., Pypno, K., Everett, J. A. C., Białek, M., & Gawronski, B. (2021). The Drunk Utilitarian Revisited: Does Alcohol Really Increase Utilitarianism in Moral Judgment? Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 49(1), 20–31. https://doi.org/10.1177/01461672211052120
effects.title_original: The drunk utilitarian: Blood alcohol concentration predicts utilitarian responses in moral dilemmas
effects.title_replication: The Drunk Utilitarian Revisited: Does Alcohol Really Increase Utilitarianism in Moral Judgment?

effects.description: For auditory flankers, correct responses are significantly faster in the perceptual-congruent condition than in the response-congruent and incongruent conditions, with no difference in response times between the latter two conditions (interaction of congruency and flanker modality)
effects.outcome_quote: In summary, we found significant evidence for bimodal Flanker effects, F(2, 292) = 83.94, p < .001, np2 = .37, and a significant interaction of congruency and flanker modality, F(2, 292) = 4.28, p = .015, np2 = .03.
effects.ref_original: Ulrich, R., Prislan, L., & Miller, J. (2020). A bimodal extension of the Eriksen flanker task. Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics, 83(2), 790–799. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13414-020-02150-8
effects.ref_replication: Papenmeier, F., & Rebholz, T. R. (2023, September). Bimodal Flanker: Audio vs. Visual. Retrieved from https://osf.io/8nzfm/
effects.title_original: A bimodal extension of the Eriksen flanker task
effects.title_replication: Bimodal Flanker: Audio vs. Visual

effects.description: In this paper, the authors offer insight into a previously documented observation known as the left-digit bias, whereby consumers tend to give greater weight to the left-most digit when comparing two prices. This means, for example, that consumers tend to treat a price difference of $4.00 vs. $2.99 as larger than $4.01 vs. $3.00. The authors’ key claim is that this bias is greater when the prices are presented side-by-side, in a way that makes them easier to compare, than sequentially, on two separate but consecutive screens.
effects.outcome_quote: We find marginally significant support for the authors’ key interaction in both replications. [...] Although the evidence is much weaker than in the original study, we are inclined to believe that the original effect is real.
effects.ref_original: Sokolova, T., Seenivasan, S., & Thomas, M. (2020). The Left-Digit Bias: When and Why Are Consumers Penny Wise and Pound Foolish? Journal of Marketing Research, 57(4), 771–788. https://doi.org/10.1177/0022243720932532
effects.ref_replication: Simmons, J. &. Nelson, L.D. (2020). [92] Data Replicada #8: Is The Left-Digit Bias Stronger When Prices Are Presented Side-By-Side? Data Colada.
effects.title_original: The Left-Digit Bias: When and Why Are Consumers Penny Wise and Pound Foolish?
effects.title_replication: [92] Data Replicada #8: Is The Left-Digit Bias Stronger When Prices Are Presented Side-By-Side?

effects.description: Concerns about disease, pathogens, germs can bias people toward categorizing social targets as members of an unfamiliar out-group
effects.outcome_quote: The current findings replicated part but not all of the pattern observed in the original study. [...] Considered together, findings bolster support for the overall hypothesis that pathogen threat is associated with biased social categorization of targets displaying heuristic cues to disease.
effects.ref_original: Makhanova, A., Miller, S. L., & Maner, J. K. (2015). Germs and the out-group: Chronic and situational disease concerns affect intergroup categorization. Evolutionary Behavioral Sciences, 9(1), 8–19. https://doi.org/10.1037/ebs0000028

effects.description: A rule learning effect such that after a brief auditory exposure phase, 7-month-old infants were able to learn and generalize a rule to novel syllables not previously present in the exposure phase.
effects.outcome_quote: Our results did not replicate the original findings: infants showed no difference in looking time between test patterns consistent or inconsistent with the familiarization pattern they were exposed to.
effects.ref_original: Marcus, G. F., Vijayan, S., Bandi Rao, S., & Vishton, P. M. (1999). Rule Learning by Seven-Month-Old Infants. Science, 283(5398), 77–80. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.283.5398.77

effects.title_original: Rule Learning by Seven-Month-Old Infants
effects.title_replication: Robustness of the rule‐learning effect in 7‐month‐old infants: A close, multicenter replication of Marcus et al. (1999)

effects.description: Perceiving four varieties of vegetables (carrots, broccoli, cauliflower, tomatoes) as relatively scarce will 1. increase relative preferences for individuals’ favorite of the four 2. Lead to individuals’ favorite vegetable being chosen more from a choice in which participants can select six pieces of vegetable.
effects.outcome_quote: Results suggested that there was no significant difference between the scarce (M = 2.92, SD = 0.96) and abundant (M = 2.86, SD = 1.04) conditions on the number of different vegetables chosen, ZMann–Whitney = -0.22, p = 0.83.
effects.ref_original: Zhu, M., & Ratner, R. K. (2015). Scarcity Polarizes Preferences: The Impact on Choice among Multiple Items in a Product Class. Journal of Marketing Research, 52(1), 13–26. https://doi.org/10.1509/jmr.13.0451
effects.ref_replication: O’Donnell, M., Dev, A. S., Antonoplis, S., Baum, S. M., Benedetti, A. H., Brown, N. D., Carrillo, B., Choi, A. L., Connor, P., Donnelly, K., Ellwood-Lowe, M. E., Foushee, R., Jansen, R., Jarvis, S. N., Lundell-Creagh, R., Ocampo, J. M., Okafor, G. N., Azad, Z. R., Rosenblum, M., … Nelson, L. D. (2021). Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
effects.title_original: Scarcity Polarizes Preferences: The Impact on Choice among Multiple Items in a Product Class
effects.title_replication: Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity

effects.description: Unfavorable consumer perceptions and behavioral outcomes will be highest when the triple scarcity effect occurs (i.e., no liquidity, limited lending options, and perceived consequences of loss).
effects.outcome_quote: We submitted participants responses to an ANOVA predicting the amount participants borrowed from their condition assignment (all three factors plus their interaction), which returned a significant three-way interaction, (F(1, 497) = 9.75, p = 0.002, partial2p= .019).

effects.description: The mere presence of one’s own smartphone may occupy limited-capacity cognitive resources, thereby leaving fewer resources available for other tasks and undercutting cognitive performance
effects.outcome_quote: Using the same tasks and conditions as in Ward et al.'s (2017) second experiment, the present study found that the “brain drain” effect did not replicate: there was no difference between smartphone location conditions on performance on either the o-span task or the go/no-go task.
effects.ref_original: Ward, A. F., Duke, K., Gneezy, A., & Bos, M. W. (2017). Brain Drain: The Mere Presence of One’s Own Smartphone Reduces Available Cognitive Capacity. Journal of the Association for Consumer Research, 2(2), 140–154. https://doi.org/10.1086/691462
effects.ref_replication: Ruiz Pardo, A. C., & Minda, J. P. (2022). Reexamining the “brain drain” effect: A replication of Ward et al. (2017). Acta Psychologica, 230, 103717. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2022.103717

effects.description: By manipulating the timing of an activity, the Time-Elicit (TEL) procedure of stating the maximum amount of hours required for a task should be more sensitive to a change in time scarcity, because time of money required for a task). Analogously, the TEL procedure should be more sensitive to a change in the timing of the activity. That is, distant activities scarcity receives a higher weight in TEL vs. Money-Elicit (MEL) procedure - stating the minimum amount should yield lower wage rates, particularly in the TEL (vs. MEL) procedure, which should diminish the wage rate asymmetry (effect of elicitation procedure)
effects.outcome_quote: A main effect of procedure emerged, such that the desired wage rate was higher in the TEL (vs.MEL) condition (MTEL = $15.06, SD = $9.54; MMEL = $8.71, SD = $8.21; F(1, 463) = 27.26, p < .001, Cohen’s d = 0.179). There was no significant difference of the wage rate in the distant versus near future (Mnear future = $12.44, SD = $10.63; Mdistant future = $11.41, SD = $8.14; F(1, 463) =0.32, p = 0.6, Cohen’s d = .028). Additionally, there was no significant two- way interaction between procedure and timin

effects.description: Unearned luxury consumption will lead to lower brand attitude than earned luxury consumption when consumers' fairness concerns are salient, but less so when fairness concerns are not salient (effect of consumption resources)
effects.outcome_quote: Only the effect of consumption resources was significant, F(2, 598) = 4.28, p = .014, ηp2 = .014, η2 = .014; its interaction with fairness was NS, F(2, 598) = 1.05, p = .351, ηp2 = .003, η2 = .003; the effect of fairness was NS, F(1, 598) = 1.01, p = .315, ηp2 = .002, η2 = .002. The two- way interaction remained NS when each background measure (e.g., familiarity with the brands) was included as a control variable.
effects.ref_original: Lee, S., Baumgartner, H., & Winterich, K. P. (2018). Did They Earn It? Observing Unearned Luxury Consumption Decreases Brand Attitude When Observers Value Fairness. Journal of Consumer Psychology, 28(3), 412–436. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/jcpy.1028

effects.description: Unearned luxury consumption will lead to lower brand attitude than earned luxury consumption when consumers' fairness concerns are salient, but less so when fairness concerns are not salient (attitude toward the brands in the unspecified vs. unearned when fairness was not salient)
effects.outcome_quote: Only the effect of consumption resources was significant, F(2, 598) = 4.28, p = .014, ηp2 = .014, η2 = .014; its interaction with fairness was NS, F(2, 598) = 1.05, p = .351, ηp2 = .003, η2 = .003; the effect of fairness was NS, F(1, 598) = 1.01, p = .315, ηp2 = .002, η2 = .002. The two- way interaction remained NS when each background measure (e.g., familiarity with the brands) was included as a control variable.
effects.ref_original: Lee, S., Baumgartner, H., & Winterich, K. P. (2018). Did They Earn It? Observing Unearned Luxury Consumption Decreases Brand Attitude When Observers Value Fairness. Journal of Consumer Psychology, 28(3), 412–436. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/jcpy.1028

effects.description: Participants who encounter scarcity salience in a prior unrelated context would be more likely to use the provided products in novel but appropriate ways to solve the given task (effect of resource availability on the correctness of the solutions)
effects.outcome_quote: A chi-square test revealed no significant effect of resource availability on the correctness of the solutions (X2 (2, N = 319) = 2.224, p = .329). We next conducted a binary logistic regression analysis to assess the differences between the conditions. The results showed that there were no significant differences between the scarcity condition (M=27%) and the abundance condition (M = 29%, B = -.02, [SE] = .06, Wald = .063, p = .802), and that there was no significant difference between the scarcity condition and the control condition (M = 36%, B = -.09, [SE] = .06, Wald = 1.90, p = .174.) Furthermore, we observed no difference between the abundance and control conditions (B = -.07, [SE] = .06, Wald = 1.2, p = .278). Including careless responders did not qualitatively change the key study result: there was still no significant difference between the scarcity and control condition (M = 36%, B = -.10, [SE] = .06, Wald = 2.422, p = .120).
effects.ref_original: Mehta, R., & Zhu, M. (2015). Creating When You Have Less: The Impact of Resource Scarcity on Product Use Creativity. Journal of Consumer Research, 42(5), 767–782. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucv051

effects.description: This study predicts that there will be higher preferences for violent experiences after exposure to the limited-quantity scarcity promotion (vs. control) among participants who did not reduce the competitive threat via the affiliation task (perceived competitive threat: high). This study also predicts an attenuation of the scarcity-aggression link among participants who did reduce the competitive threat via the affiliation task (perceived competitive threat: low) (differences between scarcity and control ads in high-threat condition)
effects.outcome_quote: The ANOVA revealed no effect of perceived threat (PHigh Threat = .30, SD = .24 vs. PLow Threat = .34, SD = .26, F(1, 385) = 2.65, p = .105, partial eta- 42 squared = .007) and no effect of scarcity promotion (PScarcity = .33, SD = .25 vs. PControl = .32, SD = .25, F(1, 385) = 0.04, p = .843, partial eta-squared = .000). In addition, the hypothesized interaction was not significant (F(1, 385) = 0.01, p = .945, partial eta-squared = .000).
effects.ref_original: Kristofferson, K., McFerran, B., Morales, A. C., & Dahl, D. W. (2016). The Dark Side of Scarcity Promotions: How Exposure to Limited-Quantity Promotions Can Induce Aggression. Journal of Consumer Research, ucw056. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucw056

effects.description: In times of greater economic scarcity, the preference for giving resources to girls (vs. boys) will be moderated by individual differences in risk aversion with greater differences between economic conditions in resource allocation to girls (vs boys) for participants with higher levels of trait risk aversion (effect of economic condition)
effects.outcome_quote: While the replication did find a significant interaction, it was not in the same direction and the original effect size was not included in the replication 95% confidence interval.
effects.ref_original: Durante, K. M., Griskevicius, V., Redden, J. P., & Edward White, A. (2015). Spending on Daughters versus Sons in Economic Recessions. Journal of Consumer Research, 42(3), 435–457. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucv023

effects.description: Recalling an instance in which one felt economically scarce causes an increase in reported physical pain
effects.outcome_quote: The results of an independent samples t-test indicated that participants who recalled an economically insecure period in their life reported more physical pain (M = 1.57, SD = 2.14) than participants who recalled an economically secure period (M = 0.78, SD = 1.49, t(601) = 5.24, p < .001, Cohen’s d = 0.43, 95% confidence interval (CI) = [0.27, 0.59]. This effect did not remain significant after we controlled for participants’ age, gender, current employment status, and negative affect, p = .08. Including careless responders (N = 45) did not change qualitative results: participants who recalled an economically insecure period in their life reported more physical pain (M = 1.94, SD = 2.57) than participants who recalled an economically secure period (M = 1.06, SD = 1.99, t(656) = 4.89, p < .001, Cohen’s d = 0.38, 95% confidence interval (CI) = [0.26, 0.56]) and this effect did not remain significant after we controlled for participants’ age, gender, current employment status, and negative affect, p = .14.
effects.ref_original: Chou, E. Y., Parmar, B. L., & Galinsky, A. D. (2016). Economic Insecurity Increases Physical Pain. Psychological Science, 27(4), 443–454. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797615625640

effects.description: The mere presence of one’s own smartphone may occupy limited-capacity cognitive resources, thereby leaving fewer resources available for other tasks and undercutting cognitive performance
effects.outcome_quote: Using the same tasks and conditions as in Ward et al.'s (2017) second experiment, the present study found that the “brain drain” effect did not replicate: there was no difference between smartphone location conditions on performance on either the o-span task or the go/no-go task.

effects.description: 1a
effects.outcome_quote: We conducted two pre-registered replication studies (total: N = 3302, MTurk/Prolific) of Study 1 in Heyman and Ariely (2004) and found support for one of their main claims that people are more sensitive to worth when the reward is cash than goods. However, the rewards’ monetary worth impacted expected willingness to help even in social markets, deviating from the original findings.
effects.ref_original: Heyman, J., & Ariely, D. (2004). Effort for Payment. Psychological Science, 15(11), 787–793. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.0956-7976.2004.00757.x

effects.title_replication: Rewarding More Is Better for Soliciting Help, Yet More So for Cash Than for Goods: Revisiting and Reframing the Tale of Two Markets With Replications and Extensions of Heyman and Ariely (2004)

effects.description: The discrepancy between actual and estimated consumption among participants using self-refilling bowls.
effects.outcome_quote: The results of the present study did replicate the key finding from Wansink et al.—participants that ate from the imperceptibly self-refilling bowl ate more than those eating from the regular bowl. [...] (32% more soup consumption, or d = 0.45) [...] In our preregistration, we had indicated that a successful replication would be defined as “If our results show that the experimental (biased visual cue) group consumed significantly more soup than the control (accurate visual cue) group, but their estimated consumption and satiation ratings were not significantly different between groups. In terms of the size of the effect, we will consider the effect replicated if the difference in the two groups in terms of consumption is at least half of the original effect (i.e., Cohen’s d = 0.4223).” While participants in the control group estimated that they ate significantly more soup than did those in the experimental group, these results are still consistent with Wansink and colleagues’ hypotheses. We therefore deem this replication successful.
effects.ref_original: Wansink, B., Painter, J. E., & North, J. (2005). Bottomless Bowls: Why Visual Cues of Portion Size May Influence Intake**. Obesity Research, 13(1), 93–100. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1038/oby.2005.12
effects.ref_replication: Lopez, A., Choi, A. K., Dellawar, N. C., Cullen, B. C., Avila Contreras, S., Rosenfeld, D. L., & Tomiyama, A. J. (2024). Visual cues and food intake: A preregistered replication of Wansink et al. (2005). Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 153(2), 275–281. https://doi.org/10.1037/xge0001503
effects.title_original: Bottomless Bowls: Why Visual Cues of Portion Size May Influence Intake**
effects.title_replication: Visual cues and food intake: A preregistered replication of Wansink et al. (2005).

effects.description: A more extreme comparison of the very-youngest and very-oldest participants in the sample.
effects.outcome_quote: We replicate the difference in age estimates, but find no relationship between participants’ age and their perception of the ambiguous figure. This leads us to conclude that the positive relationship between participants’ age and their age estimates of the ambiguous ‘my wife/mother-in-law’ figure is better explained by the own-age anchor effect [...]. Our results disqualify the original finding as an example of cognitive penetrability: the participants’ age biases their judgment of the ambiguous figure, not its perception.
effects.ref_original: Nicholls, M. E. R., Churches, O., & Loetscher, T. (2018). Perception of an ambiguous figure is affected by own-age social biases. Scientific Reports, 8(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-31129-7
effects.ref_replication: Brouwer, A., Jin, X., Waldi, A. H., & Verheyen, S. (2021). Age biases the judgment rather than the perception of an ambiguous figure. Scientific Reports, 11(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-88139-1
effects.title_original: Perception of an ambiguous figure is affected by own-age social biases
effects.title_replication: Age biases the judgment rather than the perception of an ambiguous figure

effects.description: The overall impact of emotion on body sway during exposure to social threat.

effects.description: people perceive their risk of experiencing a negative health/safety hazard as less likely than their counterparts
effects.outcome_quote: In the current replication study, we found support for the original findings in the target article for comparative risk judgments, yet with weaker effects (Cohen’s d = -0.40, 95% CI [-0.59, -0.22]). Additionally, the current replication also found support for the Optimism bias effect across five risk categories (1. Actions / 2. Heredity / 3. Physical/physiological / 4. Environmental / 5.Psychlogical in all 12 of the hypothetical health and safety scenarios.
effects.ref_original: Weinstein, N. D. (1984). Why it won’t happen to me: Perceptions of risk factors and susceptibility. Health Psychology, 3(5), 431–457. https://doi.org/10.1037/0278-6133.3.5.431

effects.title_original: Why it won't happen to me: Perceptions of risk factors and susceptibility.
effects.title_replication: Weinstein (1984): Replication and extension

effects.description: people tended to regard more positive traits as more descriptive of themselves than of the average other
effects.outcome_quote: We successfully replicated the trait desirability effect, such that participants rated more desirable traits as being more descriptive of themselves than of others (original: [Formula: see text] = .78, 95% confidence interval [CI] [.73, .81]; replication: sr 2 = .54, 95% CI [.43, .65]). The effect of desirability was stronger for more controllable traits (effect of Desirability × Controllability interaction on self–other-ratings difference; original: [Formula: see text] = .21, 95% CI [.12, .28]; replication: sr 2 = .07, 95% CI [.02, .12]). [...] Thirty-five years later, the better-than-average effect appears to remain robust.
effects.ref_original: Alicke, M. D. (1985). Global self-evaluation as determined by the desirability and controllability of trait adjectives. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 49(6), 1621–1630. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.49.6.1621
effects.ref_replication: Ziano, I., Mok, P. Y. (Cora), & Feldman, G. (2020). Replication and Extension of Alicke (1985) Better-Than-Average Effect for Desirable and Controllable Traits. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 12(6), 1005–1017. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550620948973
effects.title_original: Global self-evaluation as determined by the desirability and controllability of trait adjectives.
effects.title_replication: Replication and Extension of Alicke (1985) Better-Than-Average Effect for Desirable and Controllable Traits
effects.journal_original: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology

effects.description: Macbeth effect ([im]moral act…
effects.outcome_quote: We were unsuccessful in replicating the Zhong & Liljenquist ́s results that supported cleansing as a psychological mechanism for compensating guilty: results couldn’t confirm an increased mental accessibility of cleansing-related concepts or even a greater desire for cleansing products, neither a greater likelihood of taking antiseptic wipes.
effects.ref_original: Zhong, C.-B., & Liljenquist, K. (2006). Washing Away Your Sins: Threatened Morality and Physical Cleansing. Science, 313(5792), 1451–1452. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1130726
effects.ref_replication: Gámez, E., Díaz, J. M., & Marrero, H. (2011). The Uncertain Universality of the Macbeth Effect with a Spanish Sample. The Spanish Journal of Psychology, 14(1), 156–162. https://doi.org/10.5209/rev_sjop.2011.v14.n1.13

effects.description: Due to perceptions of (in)appropriateness, the use of smileys may backfire and produce less favorable perceptions of competence in a formal work-related setting, yet more favorable perceptions of warmth in an informal work-related setting.
effects.outcome_quote: The authors of the original study reported support for their proposition that [...] the use of smileys may backfire and produce less favorable perceptions of competence in a formal work-related setting, yet more favorable perceptions of warmth in an informal work-related setting. Our results, in contrast, indicated that smileys produce a negative effect on perceptions of competence and a positive effect on perceptions of warmth, regardless of the level of formality.
effects.ref_original: Glikson, E., Cheshin, A., & Kleef, G. A. van. (2017). The Dark Side of a Smiley. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 9(5), 614–625. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550617720269
effects.ref_replication: Lai, L., & Mayiwar, L. (2023). The Dark Versus Bright Side of a Smiley: A Preregistered Replication of Experiment 3 in Glikson et al. (2018) “The Dark Side of a Smiley.” Collabra: Psychology, 9(1). https://doi.org/10.1525/collabra.90195
effects.title_original: The Dark Side of a Smiley
effects.title_replication: The Dark Versus Bright Side of a Smiley: A Preregistered Replication of Experiment 3 in Glikson et al. (2018) “The Dark Side of a Smiley”

effects.description: Due to perceptions of (in)appropriateness, the use of smileys may backfire and produce less favorable perceptions of competence in a formal work-related setting, yet more favorable perceptions of warmth in an informal work-related setting.
effects.outcome_quote: The authors of the original study reported support for their proposition that [...] the use of smileys may backfire and produce less favorable perceptions of competence in a formal work-related setting, yet more favorable perceptions of warmth in an informal work-related setting. Our results, in contrast, indicated that smileys produce a negative effect on perceptions of competence and a positive effect on perceptions of warmth, regardless of the level of formality.
effects.ref_original: Glikson, E., Cheshin, A., & Kleef, G. A. van. (2017). The Dark Side of a Smiley. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 9(5), 614–625. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550617720269
effects.ref_replication: Lai, L., & Mayiwar, L. (2023). The Dark Versus Bright Side of a Smiley: A Preregistered Replication of Experiment 3 in Glikson et al. (2018) “The Dark Side of a Smiley.” Collabra: Psychology, 9(1). https://doi.org/10.1525/collabra.90195

effects.description: Standing, compared to sitting, enhances selective attention by reducing the Stroop interference effect.
effects.outcome_quote: All experiments yielded the standard Stroop effect, but we failed to detect any consistent effect of posture (sitting vs. standing) on the magnitude of the Stroop effect.

effects.description: Standing, compared to sitting, enhances selective attention by reducing the Stroop interference effect.

effects.description: Low likehood have effect on abstract construal (Trial 2)
effects.outcome_quote: Contrary to the original empirical findings, we present 2 close replication attempts (N 115 and N 120; the original studies had N 20 and N 34) that failed to find the effect of likelihood on construal level. Bayesian analyses provided diagnostic support for the absence of an effect.
effects.ref_original: Wakslak, C. J., Trope, Y., Liberman, N., & Alony, R. (2006). Seeing the forest when entry is unlikely: Probability and the mental representation of events. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 135(4), 641–653. https://doi.org/10.1037/0096-3445.135.4.641

effects.outcome_quote: "we set out to replicate and extend Owen et al.'s findings regarding the interaction between psychodynamic‐interpersonal (PI) technique, cognitive‐behavioural (CB) technique and the alliance with patient rated change [...] We also found interactions between both PI and CB technique use with alliance, in predicting patient rated change."

effects.description: Need for dominence / desire for status is positively associated with overconfidence
effects.outcome_quote: We found support for the status-enhancement hypothesis: Desire for status had a positive association with overconfidence using both the original measure of desire for status (β = 0.19, 95% [0.09, 0.28]) and the alternative measure (β = 0.31, 95% [0.22, 0.39]).
effects.ref_original: Anderson, C., Brion, S., Moore, D. A., & Kennedy, J. A. (2012). A status-enhancement account of overconfidence. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 103(4), 718–735. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0029395

effects.description: leader brands hope young adults shop in the afternoon, but follower brands hope for morning
effects.outcome_quote: Using two experimental studies in the USA, they found variety-seeking was lower in the afternoon than in the morning. [...] Similar to Gullo et al. (2019), we found variety-seeking increases from the morning to the afternoon. [...] Our results as such lend further support to the matching mechanism [...] proposed by Gullo et al. (2019) rather than the compensating mechanism suggested by Roehm and Roehm (2004).

effects.description: People judge actions that produce harm as a by- product to be more moral than actions that produce harm directly
effects.outcome_quote: we successfully replicated Study 2 of Royzman and Baron (2002) with a Hong Kong student sample (N = 46) and an online American Mechanical Turk sample (N = 314). We found con- sistent evidential support for the preference for indirect harm phenomenon (d = 0.46 [0.26, 0.65] to 0.47 [0.18, 0.75]), weaker than effects reported in the original findings of the target article (d = 0.70 [0.40, 0.99]). We also successfully replicated findings regarding reasons underlying a preference for indirect harm (di- rectness, intent, omission, probability of harm, and appearance of harm).
effects.ref_original: Royzman, E. B., & Baron, J. (2002). The Preference for Indirect Harm. Social Justice Research, 15(2), 165–184. https://doi.org/10.1023/a:1019923923537

Perceived morality of direct versus indirect harm: Replications of the preference for indirect harm effect. Meta-Psychology, 5. https://doi.org/10.15626/mp.2019.2134
effects.title_original: The Preference for Indirect Harm
effects.title_replication: Perceived morality of direct versus indirect harm: Replications of the preference for indirect harm effect

effects.description: Participants in the hindsight condition estimate a greater probability that the outcome will continue to occur in future trials, compared with participants in the foresight condition.
effects.outcome_quote: In Study 2 (N = 608), we replicated Experiment 1 in Slovic and Fischhoff (1977), and found support for hindsight bias in prospective judgments (dmean = 0.40).

effects.description: The presence of a decoy makes the target more appealing than in its absence, this effect is reduced when regret is made salient

effects.description: Knowledge of one’s experiential purchases is perceived as providing greater insight into one’s true self than knowledge of one’s material purchases.
effects.outcome_quote: In a pre-registered experiment with a US American Amazon Mechanical Turk sample, (N=743), we successfully replicated their Studies 3A, 3B, 3C, and 5.
effects.ref_original: Carter, T. J., & Gilovich, T. (2012). I am what I do, not what I have: The differential centrality of experiential and material purchases to the self. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 102(6), 1304–1317. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0027407

effects.journal_original: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology

effects.description: increasing benefits leads people to judge its risks as lower
effects.outcome_quote: In two well-powered studies, using a more stringent analytic strategy, we replicated the original finding. We observed a strong negative relationship between judgments of risks and benefits across three technologies, although we do find that there was no change in risks when highlighting low benefits.
effects.ref_original: Finucane, M. L., Alhakami, A., Slovic, P., & Johnson, S. M. (2000). The affect heuristic in judgments of risks and benefits. Journal of Behavioral Decision Making, 13(1), 1–17. https://doi.org/10.1002/(sici)1099-0771(200001/03)13:13.0.co;2-s

Risky Therefore Not Beneficial: Replication and Extension of Finucane et al.’s (2000) Affect Heuristic Experiment. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 13(7), 1173–1184. https://doi.org/10.1177/19485506211056761
effects.title_original: The affect heuristic in judgments of risks and benefits
effects.title_replication: Risky Therefore Not Beneficial: Replication and Extension of Finucane et al.’s (2000) Affect Heuristic Experiment

effects.description: Participants overestimate how much payment changes others' willingness to donate blood.
effects.outcome_quote: We present two registered, close, and successful replications [...] Consistent with the original article, participants overestimated the impact of payment on blood donation in Study 1 [...] and overestimated the importance of smoking status for smoking policy preferences in Study 4

effects.description: people are subject to money illusion, and believe that workers would be less happy with a job and more willing to quit a job if they received a salary raise that is higher in real terms, but lower in nominal terms. The second was that describing a problem in economic terms, without asking about happiness or job attractiveness, reduces money illusion.
effects.outcome_quote: We found effect sizes in line with the original ones for assessments of income (Problem 1; original: Cramer’s V = 0.26, 95% CI [0.17, 0.37]; replication: V = 0.28 [0.21, 0.36]),
effects.ref_original: Shafir, E., Diamond, P., & Tversky, A. (1997). Money Illusion. The Quarterly Journal of Economics, 112(2), 341–374. https://doi.org/10.1162/003355397555208

effects.description: Omission will be associated with a bias towards lower attributions of immorality
effects.outcome_quote: In a pre-registered experiment (N = 313), we successfully replicated an experiment from Spranca, Minsk, and Baron (1991) [...] Participants in the harm through commission condition(s) rated harm as more immoral and attributed higher responsibility compared to participants in the harm through omission condition (d = 0.45 to 0.47 and d = 0.40 to 0.53).
effects.ref_original: Spranca, M., Minsk, E., & Baron, J. (1991). Omission and commission in judgment and choice. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 27(1), 76–105. https://doi.org/10.1016/0022-1031(91)90011-t

effects.description: Viewing angry (socially threatening) faces leads to reduced body sway and decreased heart rate (freezing response) in humans.
effects.outcome_quote: Our results support the hypothesis that participants show physiological signs of freezing when looking at angry faces. [...] Like Roelofs et al., we find evidence that the effects are modulated by state anxiety, but our effects are only marginal and we do not replicate the negative correlation between heart rate and state anxiety in the angry block.
effects.ref_original: Roelofs, K., Hagenaars, M. A., & Stins, J. (2010). Facing Freeze. Psychological Science, 21(11), 1575–1581. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797610384746

effects.title_original: Facing Freeze

effects.outcome_quote: Results revealed that original looking patterns could still not be replicated.

effects.description: Visual portion size cues affect consumption behavior by shaping consumption norms and expectations, leading to unintentional overeating.
effects.outcome_quote: The results of the present study did replicate the key finding from Wansink et al.—participants that ate from the imperceptibly self-refilling bowl ate more than those eating from the regular bowl. [...] (32% more soup consumption, or d = 0.45) [...] In our preregistration, we had indicated that a successful replication would be defined as “If our results show that the experimental (biased visual cue) group consumed significantly more soup than the control (accurate visual cue) group, but their estimated consumption and satiation ratings were not significantly different between groups. In terms of the size of the effect, we will consider the effect replicated if the difference in the two groups in terms of consumption is at least half of the original effect (i.e., Cohen’s d = 0.4223).” While participants in the control group estimated that they ate significantly more soup than did those in the experimental group, these results are still consistent with Wansink and colleagues’ hypotheses. We therefore deem this replication successful.
effects.ref_original: Wansink, B., Painter, J. E., & North, J. (2005). Bottomless Bowls: Why Visual Cues of Portion Size May Influence Intake**. Obesity Research, 13(1), 93–100. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1038/oby.2005.12
effects.ref_replication: Lopez, A., Choi, A. K., Dellawar, N. C., Cullen, B. C., Avila Contreras, S., Rosenfeld, D. L., & Tomiyama, A. J. (2024). Visual cues and food intake: A preregistered replication of Wansink et al. (2005). Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 153(2), 275–281. https://doi.org/10.1037/xge0001503
effects.title_original: Bottomless Bowls: Why Visual Cues of Portion Size May Influence Intake**
effects.title_replication: Visual cues and food intake: A preregistered replication of Wansink et al. (2005).

effects.description: Observer’s own age biases subconscious perception of an ambiguous face, causing age estimates to align with the observer’s age group
effects.outcome_quote: We replicate the difference in age estimates, but find no relationship between participants’ age and their perception of the ambiguous figure. This leads us to conclude that the positive relationship between participants’ age and their age estimates of the ambiguous ‘my wife/mother-in-law’ figure is better explained by the own-age anchor effect [...]. Our results disqualify the original finding as an example of cognitive penetrability: the participants’ age biases their judgment of the ambiguous figure, not its perception.
effects.ref_original: Nicholls, M. E. R., Churches, O., & Loetscher, T. (2018). Perception of an ambiguous figure is affected by own-age social biases. Scientific Reports, 8(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-31129-7
effects.ref_replication: Brouwer, A., Jin, X., Waldi, A. H., & Verheyen, S. (2021). Age biases the judgment rather than the perception of an ambiguous figure. Scientific Reports, 11(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-88139-1
effects.title_original: Perception of an ambiguous figure is affected by own-age social biases
effects.title_replication: Age biases the judgment rather than the perception of an ambiguous figure

effects.description: Physically elevated viewpoints lead to an illusory sense of control, which increases individuals’ risk-taking behavior.
effects.outcome_quote: In sum, using three times the sample size as the original study, we find no evidence that being exposed to an elevated vertical position increases risk taking, at least when risk is operationalized as the likelihood of purchasing new products.
effects.ref_original: Jami, A. (2019). Having Control Over and Above Situations: The Influence of Elevated Viewpoints on Risk Taking. Journal of Marketing Research, 56(2), 230–244. https://doi.org/10.1177/0022243718813544
effects.ref_replication: Simmons, J. &. Nelson, L.D. (2019). [82] Data Replicada #1: Do Elevated Viewpoints Increase Risk Taking? Data Colada.
effects.title_original: Having Control Over and Above Situations: The Influence of Elevated Viewpoints on Risk Taking
effects.title_replication: [82] Data Replicada #1: Do Elevated Viewpoints Increase Risk Taking?

effects.description: Red labels make participants drink significantly less from the cups
effects.outcome_quote: "Unlike in the previous study, the findings in our replication study did not provide empirical support for the hypothesis that people will drink less from red-labeled cups than blue-labeled cups. The difference between groups in drink consumption was non-significant. Thus, the red color did not have an inhibitory effect on drink intake."

effects.description: subtle linguistic cues embedded in voter mobilization messages dramatically increase turnout
effects.outcome_quote: We find no evidence that this minor wording change increases turnout levels. This research provides new evidence that the strategy of invoking the self does not appear to consistently increase turnout and calls into question whether subtle linguistic cues have outsized behavioral effects.
effects.ref_original: Bryan, C. J., Walton, G. M., Rogers, T., & Dweck, C. S. (2011). Motivating voter turnout by invoking the self. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 108(31), 12653–12656. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1103343108

effects.title_original: Motivating voter turnout by invoking the self
effects.title_replication: Do Subtle Linguistic Interventions Priming a Social Identity as a Voter Have Outsized Effects on Voter Turnout? Evidence From a New Replication Experiment
effects.journal_original: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

effects.description: generalized trust predicts deception detection accuracy

effects.description: distortion in perceptual body image in eating disorders
effects.outcome_quote: Low-BMI control participants overestimated their size, while high-BMI controls underestimated their size, exhibiting the expected normal perceptual contraction bias. However, the women with a history of eating disorders showed no evidence of contraction bias.

effects.description: Negative News Increases Arousal and Negative Affect

effects.description: Unemployment’s influence on FFM personality traits (mean-level changes in openness, depending on the number of years unemployed)
effects.outcome_quote: Latent change analyses indicated no effect of job loss on any Big Five trait. Moderating effects of unemployment duration or gender were not found. Even analyses accounting for potential selection effects led to comparable results. Thus, personality seemed invariant despite changes in employment status.
effects.ref_original: Boyce, C. J., Wood, A. M., Daly, M., & Sedikides, C. (2015). Personality change following unemployment. Journal of Applied Psychology, 100(4), 991–1011. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0038647

effects.description: Relationship closeness leads to a desire to maintain the relationship, this desire leads to motivated reasoning, and this motivation leads to interpersonal forgiveness.

effects.description: Complete over part probe advantage are mandatory.
effects.outcome_quote: This effect is readily demonstrable with learned faces (Donnelly & Davidoff, 1990, 1999; Homa, Haver, & Schwartz, 1976; Tanaka & Farah, 1993), but has also been demonstrated with unfamiliar faces through the use of a sequential matching task rather than a recognition task (Donnelly & Davidoff, 1999, Experiment 5; Tanaka, Kiefer, & Bukach, 2004; although see Leder & Carbon, 2005, for a discussion of context effects). [...] The use of a sequential CPA task provides direct replication of Donnelly and Davidoff (1999, Experiment 5) [...] Finally, and most importantly, the analysis revealed a main effect of context, F(1, 17) = 14.45, p , .001, reflecting the expected complete-over-part advantage (CPA). Neither two-way nor three-way interactions modified these effects, all Fs(2, 34) , 1.4, p . .05. Notably, the absence of an interaction between context and familiarity meant that the CPA emerged regardless of the level of familiarity of the stimuli, F(2, 34) , 1, ns. In this sense, familiarity was not shown to influence performance in this task.
effects.ref_original: Donnelly, N., & Davidoff, J. (1999). The Mental Representations of Faces and Houses: Issues Concerning Parts and Wholes. Visual Cognition, 6(3–4), 319–343. https://doi.org/10.1080/135062899395000
effects.ref_replication: Osborne, C. D., & Stevenage, S. V. (2013). Familiarity and face processing. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 66(1), 108–120. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470218.2012.699077
effects.title_original: The Mental Representations of Faces and Houses: Issues Concerning Parts and Wholes
effects.title_replication: Familiarity and face processing

effects.description: right-handed participants perceived their [...] right hands to be longer than their left [...] hands,
effects.outcome_quote: We replicated the finding that right handers overestimate the size and grasping capacity of their right hand relative to their left hand. However, when estimates of object size and grasping capacity were made in separate tasks, objects grasped in the right hand were not underestimated relative to those grasped in the left hand. Further, when grasping capacity was physically restricted, observers appropriately recalibrated their perception of their maximum grasp but estimates of object size were unaffected.
effects.ref_original: Linkenauger, S. A., Witt, J. K., Bakdash, J. Z., Stefanucci, J. K., & Proffitt, D. R. (2009). Asymmetrical Body Perception. Psychological Science, 20(11), 1373–1380. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2009.02447.x

effects.journal_replication: Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance

effects.description: global processing broadens mental categories in memory (replication of original experiment 1; in lab)
effects.outcome_quote: Experiment 1 did not conclusively demonstrate that global priming elicited an increase in creativity; as such, the results of Experiment 1 do not match those of FD2012. Although we tested more participants per condition than did FD2012 (i.e., 50 vs. 20), it is possible that the true effect is nonzero, but smaller than reported by FD2012. In this case, a Bayes factor threshold of 10 or 1/10 may not be a realistic level of evidence to expect when testing this effect with a sample size of 100 participants.
effects.ref_original: Förster, J., & Denzler, M. (2011). RETRACTED: Sense Creative! The Impact of Global and Local Vision, Hearing, Touching, Tasting and Smelling on Creative and Analytic Thought. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 3(1), 108–117. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550611410890

effects.title_replication: Two Bayesian tests of the GLOMOsys Model.

effects.description: global processing broadens mental categories in memory (replication of original experiment 1; MTurk)

effects.description: Believer groups were better at identifying the previously defined face-like regions in the images (American sample)
effects.outcome_quote: These results suggest that the religious participants were more prone to perceive a face in everyday objects than less-religious participants, which is consistent with a past finding (Riekki et al., 2013).
effects.ref_original: Riekki, T., Lindeman, M., Aleneff, M., Halme, A., & Nuortimo, A. (2012). Paranormal and Religious Believers Are More Prone to Illusory Face Perception than Skeptics and Non‐believers. Applied Cognitive Psychology, 27(2), 150–155. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/acp.2874
effects.ref_replication: Miyazaki, Y. (2017). Being Watched by Anthropomorphized Objects Affects Charitable Donation in Religious People. Japanese Psychological Research, 59(3), 221–229. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/jpr.12158
effects.title_original: Paranormal and Religious Believers Are More Prone to Illusory Face Perception than Skeptics and Non‐believers
effects.title_replication: Being Watched by Anthropomorphized Objects Affects Charitable Donation in Religious People

effects.description: DD uniquely predicted CD symptom count, while demand did not
effects.outcome_quote: This research replicates previous outcomes relating delay discounting and demand with cannabis use and extends them by accounting for the contribution of multiple commodities.
effects.ref_original: Aston, E. R., Metrik, J., Amlung, M., Kahler, C. W., & MacKillop, J. (2016). Interrelationships between marijuana demand and discounting of delayed rewards: Convergence in behavioral economic methods. Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 169, 141–147. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.drugalcdep.2016.10.014
effects.ref_replication: Strickland, J. C., Lile, J. A., & Stoops, W. W. (2017). Unique prediction of cannabis use severity and behaviors by delay discounting and behavioral economic demand. Behavioural Processes, 140, 33–40. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.beproc.2017.03.017
effects.title_original: Interrelationships between marijuana demand and discounting of delayed rewards: Convergence in behavioral economic methods
effects.title_replication: Unique prediction of cannabis use severity and behaviors by delay discounting and behavioral economic demand

effects.description: demand uniquely predicted frequency of marijuana use while DD did not
effects.outcome_quote: This research replicates previous outcomes relating delay discounting and demand with cannabis use and extends them by accounting for the contribution of multiple commodities.
effects.ref_original: Aston, E. R., Metrik, J., Amlung, M., Kahler, C. W., & MacKillop, J. (2016). Interrelationships between marijuana demand and discounting of delayed rewards: Convergence in behavioral economic methods. Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 169, 141–147. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.drugalcdep.2016.10.014
effects.ref_replication: Strickland, J. C., Lile, J. A., & Stoops, W. W. (2017). Unique prediction of cannabis use severity and behaviors by delay discounting and behavioral economic demand. Behavioural Processes, 140, 33–40. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.beproc.2017.03.017
effects.title_original: Interrelationships between marijuana demand and discounting of delayed rewards: Convergence in behavioral economic methods
effects.title_replication: Unique prediction of cannabis use severity and behaviors by delay discounting and behavioral economic demand

effects.description: we examined whether sexualized women, but not sexualized men, are recognized in the same way as objects are (interaction)
effects.outcome_quote: It is concluded that the SBIH as postulated by Bernard et al. (2012) is based on a methodological artifact and cannot be related to SO but symmetry detection.
effects.ref_original: Bernard, P., Gervais, S. J., Allen, J., Campomizzi, S., & Klein, O. (2012). Integrating Sexual Objectification With Object Versus Person Recognition. Psychological Science, 23(5), 469–471. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611434748
effects.ref_replication: Schmidt, A. F., & Kistemaker, L. M. (2015). The sexualized-body-inversion hypothesis revisited: Valid indicator of sexual objectification or methodological artifact? Cognition, 134, 77–84. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2014.09.003
effects.title_original: Integrating Sexual Objectification With Object Versus Person Recognition
effects.title_replication: The sexualized-body-inversion hypothesis revisited: Valid indicator of sexual objectification or methodological artifact?

effects.description: we examined whether sexualized women, but not sexualized men, are recognized in the same way as objects are (post hoc)
effects.outcome_quote: It is concluded that the SBIH as postulated by Bernard et al. (2012) is based on a methodological artifact and cannot be related to SO but symmetry detection.
effects.ref_original: Bernard, P., Gervais, S. J., Allen, J., Campomizzi, S., & Klein, O. (2012). Integrating Sexual Objectification With Object Versus Person Recognition. Psychological Science, 23(5), 469–471. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611434748
effects.ref_replication: Schmidt, A. F., & Kistemaker, L. M. (2015). The sexualized-body-inversion hypothesis revisited: Valid indicator of sexual objectification or methodological artifact? Cognition, 134, 77–84. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2014.09.003
effects.title_original: Integrating Sexual Objectification With Object Versus Person Recognition
effects.title_replication: The sexualized-body-inversion hypothesis revisited: Valid indicator of sexual objectification or methodological artifact?

effects.description: cognitive strategy preferences across different levels of task-complexity and instruction (simple versus complex trials)
effects.outcome_quote: Although our results from the replication portion of the experiment mirrored those from Haun et al., data from our additional manipulations (counterbalancing the order of the egocentric and geocentric blocks for the English-speakers and as- sessing the Tseltal-speaking children’s left/right language comprehen- sion) did not favor the linguistic enculturation account.
effects.ref_original: Haun, D. B. M., Rapold, C. J., Janzen, G., & Levinson, S. C. (2011). Plasticity of human spatial cognition: Spatial language and cognition covary across cultures. Cognition, 119(1), 70–80. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2010.12.009
effects.ref_replication: Li, P., & Abarbanell, L. (2018). Competing perspectives on frames of reference in language and thought. Cognition, 170, 9–24. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2017.09.005
effects.title_original: Plasticity of human spatial cognition: Spatial language and cognition covary across cultures
effects.title_replication: Competing perspectives on frames of reference in language and thought

effects.description: cognitive strategy preferences across different levels of task-complexity and instruction (geocentric versus egocentric instructions)
effects.outcome_quote: Although our results from the replication portion of the experiment mirrored those from Haun et al., data from our additional manipulations (counterbalancing the order of the egocentric and geocentric blocks for the English-speakers and as- sessing the Tseltal-speaking children’s left/right language comprehen- sion) did not favor the linguistic enculturation account.
effects.ref_original: Haun, D. B. M., Rapold, C. J., Janzen, G., & Levinson, S. C. (2011). Plasticity of human spatial cognition: Spatial language and cognition covary across cultures. Cognition, 119(1), 70–80. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2010.12.009
effects.ref_replication: Li, P., & Abarbanell, L. (2018). Competing perspectives on frames of reference in language and thought. Cognition, 170, 9–24. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2017.09.005
effects.title_original: Plasticity of human spatial cognition: Spatial language and cognition covary across cultures
effects.title_replication: Competing perspectives on frames of reference in language and thought

effects.description: Suppression of unique neural patterns representing competing memories
effects.outcome_quote: we undertook two replication attempts of the behavioral paradigm, failing both times to observe more forgetting for competitors than targets
effects.ref_original: Wimber, M., Alink, A., Charest, I., Kriegeskorte, N., & Anderson, M. C. (2015). Retrieval induces adaptive forgetting of competing memories via cortical pattern suppression. Nature Neuroscience, 18(4), 582–589. https://doi.org/10.1038/nn.3973
effects.ref_replication: Potter, K. W., Huszar, L. D., & Huber, D. E. (2018). Does inhibition cause forgetting after selective retrieval? A reanalysis and failure to replicate. Cortex, 104, 26–45. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cortex.2018.03.026
effects.title_original: Retrieval induces adaptive forgetting of competing memories via cortical pattern suppression
effects.title_replication: Does inhibition cause forgetting after selective retrieval? A reanalysis and failure to replicate

effects.description: Airbnb hosts attractiveness on their pictures is not related to the price they charge
effects.outcome_quote: Ert, Fleischer, and Magen (2016) found that more trustworthy-looking, but not more attractive-looking, Airbnb hosts from Stockholm charge higher prices for similar apartments. [...] Our results support the general conclusion that people rely on profile photos in online markets, though we find that attractiveness is more important than trust- worthiness.
effects.ref_original: Ert, E., Fleischer, A., & Magen, N. (2016). Trust and reputation in the sharing economy: The role of personal photos in Airbnb. Tourism Management, 55, 62–73. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tourman.2016.01.013
effects.ref_replication: Jaeger, B., Sleegers, W. W. A., Evans, A. M., Stel, M., & van Beest, I. (2019). The effects of facial attractiveness and trustworthiness in online peer-to-peer markets. Journal of Economic Psychology, 75, 102125. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joep.2018.11.004
effects.title_original: Trust and reputation in the sharing economy: The role of personal photos in Airbnb
effects.title_replication: The effects of facial attractiveness and trustworthiness in online peer-to-peer markets

effects.description: 5-year-olds showed some sensitivity to proportion, and adults focused exclusively on proportion.
effects.outcome_quote: This result replicates the findings of McCrink et al. (2010) and suggests that children’s tendency to favor absolute amount over proportion persists until after the age of 5.
effects.ref_original: McCrink, K., Bloom, P., & Santos, L. R. (2009). Children’s and adults’ judgments of equitable resource distributions. Developmental Science, 13(1), 37–45. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-7687.2009.00859.x
effects.ref_replication: Ng, R., Heyman, G. D., & Barner, D. (2011). Collaboration promotes proportional reasoning about resource distribution in young children. Developmental Psychology, 47(5), 1230–1238. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0024923
effects.title_original: Children's and adults' judgments of equitable resource distributions
effects.title_replication: Collaboration promotes proportional reasoning about resource distribution in young children.

effects.description: Dogs show more person-oriented behaviours (looking, approaching, touching) when a human is crying than when the human is talking or humming.
effects.outcome_quote: Like Custance and Mayer, dogs showed more behaviours directed toward the person crying, whether the owner or the stranger, than during baseline or laughing conditions.
effects.ref_original: Custance, D., & Mayer, J. (2012). Empathic-like responding by domestic dogs (Canis familiaris) to distress in humans: an exploratory study. Animal Cognition, 15(5), 851–859. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-012-0510-1
effects.ref_replication: Meyers-Manor, J. E., & Botten, M. L. (2020). A shoulder to cry on: Heart rate variability and empathetic behavioral responses to crying and laughing in dogs. Canadian Journal of Experimental Psychology / Revue Canadienne de Psychologie Expérimentale, 74(3), 235–243. https://doi.org/10.1037/cep0000225
effects.title_original: Empathic-like responding by domestic dogs (Canis familiaris) to distress in humans: an exploratory study
effects.title_replication: A shoulder to cry on: Heart rate variability and empathetic behavioral responses to crying and laughing in dogs.
effects.journal_replication: Canadian Journal of Experimental Psychology / Revue canadienne de psychologie expérimentale

effects.description: Group therapy participants had more "not on track" alerts than individual therapy participants
effects.outcome_quote: he current study replicated the significant difference found in the rate of NOT alerts between treatment formats (p ! .007). Additionally, the timing of NOT alerts created a more complex picture. Burlingame et al.’s (2018) results may be more common as preliminary results suggest that clients in group therapy are more likely to alert as NOT during the course of therapy when compared to clients in individual therapy.
effects.ref_original: Burlingame, G. M., Whitcomb, K. E., Woodland, S. C., Olsen, J. A., Beecher, M., & Gleave, R. (2018). The effects of relationship and progress feedback in group psychotherapy using the Group Questionnaire and Outcome Questionnaire–45: A randomized clinical trial. Psychotherapy, 55(2), 116–131. https://doi.org/10.1037/pst0000133
effects.ref_replication: Alldredge, C., Burlingame, G., Olsen, J., & Van Epps, J. (2020). Outcome Questionnaire-45 (OQ-45) progress alert rates in group versus individual treatment: An archival replication. Group Dynamics: Theory, Research, and Practice, 24(4), 247–260. https://doi.org/10.1037/gdn0000121
effects.title_original: The effects of relationship and progress feedback in group psychotherapy using the Group Questionnaire and Outcome Questionnaire–45: A randomized clinical trial.
effects.title_replication: Outcome Questionnaire-45 (OQ-45) progress alert rates in group versus individual treatment: An archival replication.
effects.journal_replication: Group Dynamics: Theory, Research, and Practice

effects.description: Reminders of one's own mortality (mortality salience) increase preference for individuals who uphold cultural worldviews (e.g., pro-American essay authors) over those who challenge them (e.g., anti-American essay authors).
effects.outcome_quote: we were unable to replicate the finding under any conditions.
effects.ref_original: Greenberg, J., Pyszczynski, T., Solomon, S., Simon, L., & Breus, M. (1994). Role of consciousness and accessibility of death-related thoughts in mortality salience effects. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 67(4), 627–637. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.67.4.627
effects.ref_replication: Klein, R. A., Cook, C. L., Ebersole, C. R., Vitiello, C., Nosek, B. A., Hilgard, J., Ahn, P. H., Brady, A. J., Chartier, C. R., Christopherson, C. effects.title_replication: Many Labs 4: Failure to Replicate Mortality Salience Effect With and Without Original Author Involvement
effects.journal_original: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
