effects.description: Social Simon effect, where partner's presence triggers spatial compatibility effects (faster match/slower mismatch), vanishes when partners are distant.
effects.outcome_quote: Here, we [...] report a study in which the authors failed to replicate their key finding.
effects.ref_original: Guagnano, D., Rusconi, E., & Umiltà, C. A. (2010). Sharing a task or sharing space? On the effect of the confederate in action coding in a detection task. Cognition, 114(3), 348–355. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2009.10.008
effects.ref_replication: Welsh, T. N., Kiernan, D., Neyedli, H. F., Ray, M., Pratt, J., Potruff, A., & Weeks, D. J. (2013). Joint Simon Effects in Extrapersonal Space. Journal of Motor Behavior, 45(1), 1–5. https://doi.org/10.1080/00222895.2012.746635
effects.title_original: Sharing a task or sharing space? On the effect of the confederate in action coding in a detection task
effects.title_replication: Joint Simon Effects in Extrapersonal Space

effects.description: In a monetary incentive encoding task, cues signaled high- or low-value reward for memorizing an upcoming scene. When tested 24 hr postscan, subjects were significantly more likely to remember scenes that followed cues for high-value rather than low-value reward.
effects.outcome_quote: This replicates Adcock and colleagues’ (2006) finding that intentional memory formation is boosted by the anticipation of uncertain remote rewards
effects.ref_original: Adcock, R. A., Thangavel, A., Whitfield-Gabrieli, S., Knutson, B., & Gabrieli, J. D. E. (2006). Reward-Motivated Learning: Mesolimbic Activation Precedes Memory Formation. Neuron, 50(3), 507–517. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2006.03.036
effects.ref_replication: Spaniol, J., Schain, C., & Bowen, H. J. (2013). Reward-Enhanced Memory in Younger and Older Adults. The Journals of Gerontology Series B: Psychological Sciences and Social Sciences, 69(5), 730–740. https://doi.org/10.1093/geronb/gbt044
effects.title_original: Reward-Motivated Learning: Mesolimbic Activation Precedes Memory Formation
effects.title_replication: Reward-Enhanced Memory in Younger and Older Adults
effects.journal_replication: The Journals of Gerontology Series B: Psychological Sciences and Social Sciences

effects.description: Affect misattribution is observed even if participants are warned against it.
effects.outcome_quote: We find that although the average AMP effect remains positive, it decreases significantly in magnitude. Moreover, this reduction in the mean AMP effect under incentives masks large heterogeneity: one subset of individuals continues to experience the ‘full’ AMP effect, while another reduces their effect to approximately zero.
effects.ref_original: Payne, B. K., Cheng, C. M., Govorun, O., & Stewart, B. D. (2005). An inkblot for attitudes: Affect misattribution as implicit measurement. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 89(3), 277–293. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.89.3.277
effects.ref_replication: Hazlett, C. J., & Berinsky, A. J. (2017). Stress‐testing the affect misattribution procedure: Heterogeneous control of affect misattribution procedure effects under incentives. British Journal of Social Psychology, 57(1), 61–74. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/bjso.12217
effects.title_original: An inkblot for attitudes: Affect misattribution as implicit measurement.
effects.title_replication: Stress‐testing the affect misattribution procedure: Heterogeneous control of affect misattribution procedure effects under incentives
effects.journal_original: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology

effects.description: Presenting a living animal in a meat advertisement increased empathy and reduced willingness to eat meat.
effects.outcome_quote: In two studies, we replicated and extended past work, showing that visual reminders of meat’s animal origins (vs. images of meat alone) decreased meat consumption willingness via increased empathy for animals, distress about meat consumption, and disgust for meat.
effects.ref_original: Kunst, J. R., & Hohle, S. M. (2016). Meat eaters by dissociation: How we present, prepare and talk about meat increases willingness to eat meat by reducing empathy and disgust. Appetite, 105, 758–774. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.appet.2016.07.009
effects.ref_replication: Earle, M., Hodson, G., Dhont, K., & MacInnis, C. (2019). Eating with our eyes (closed): Effects of visually associating animals with meat on antivegan/vegetarian attitudes and meat consumption willingness. Group Processes & Intergroup Relations, 22(6), 818–835. https://doi.org/10.1177/1368430219861848
effects.title_original: Meat eaters by dissociation: How we present, prepare and talk about meat increases willingness to eat meat by reducing empathy and disgust
effects.title_replication: Eating with our eyes (closed): Effects of visually associating animals with meat on antivegan/vegetarian attitudes and meat consumption willingness

effects.description: Relative to community members, officers provided more cues and referred more often to verbal contradictions and active detection strategies when asked about beliefs.
effects.outcome_quote: Regardless of the focus of Q2, and in line with prior research, behavioral cues were reported less often, and contextual indicators more often, in responding to Q2 than in responding to Q1. However, contrary to prior fi ndings, behavioral cues still predominated in the responses to Q2.
effects.ref_original: Masip, J., & Herrero, C. (2014). Police Detection of Deception: Beliefs About Behavioral Cues to Deception Are Strong Even Though Contextual Evidence Is More Useful. Journal of Communication, 65(1), 125–145. https://doi.org/10.1111/jcom.12135
effects.ref_replication: Sánchez, N., & Masip, J. (2020). Do people detect deception the way they think they do? Replication and extensions. Psicothema, 3(32), 329–336. https://doi.org/10.7334/psicothema2019.405
effects.title_original: Police Detection of Deception: Beliefs About Behavioral Cues to Deception Are Strong Even Though Contextual Evidence Is More Useful
effects.title_replication: Do people detect deception the way they think they do? Replication and extensions

effects.description: Those who exercised in online teams (n=80) exercised significantly longer (time=78.8s, P<.001) than those who worked individually (n=35).
effects.outcome_quote: Newly collected data corroborated Irwin et al.’s (2013) finding that encouragement using the ambiguous and exclusive pronoun “you” (e.g. “you can do it”) from a virtually present partner significantly weakened this Köhler effect. Some partial support was found for Irwin et al.’s expla- nation of this effect. Irwin et al. suggested that when one hears one’s partner say things like “you can do it!”, that may be interpreted that the partner is him/herself struggling and is to some degree trying to encourage him/herself. Hence, that partner may not be as superior as previously thought.
effects.ref_original: Irwin, B. C., Feltz, D. L., & Kerr, N. L. (2013). Silence is Golden: Effect of Encouragement in Motivating the Weak Link in an Online Exercise Video Game. Journal of Medical Internet Research, 15(6), e104. https://doi.org/10.2196/jmir.2551
effects.ref_replication: Max, E. J., Feltz, D. L., Kerr, N. L., & Wittenbaum, G. M. (2016). Is silence really golden? Effect of encouragement from a partner or trainer on active video game play. International Journal of Sport and Exercise Psychology, 16(3), 261–275. https://doi.org/10.1080/1612197x.2016.1199580
effects.title_original: Silence is Golden: Effect of Encouragement in Motivating the Weak Link in an Online Exercise Video Game
effects.title_replication: Is silence really golden? Effect of encouragement from a partner or trainer on active video game play
effects.journal_replication: International Journal of Sport and Exercise Psychology

effects.description: DNA methylation levels are associated with reports of childhood abuse (physical or sexual)
effects.outcome_quote: Replicating prior work, we found that a significant effect of childhood sex abuse on methylation of the 5HTT promoter region emerged for women.
effects.ref_original: Beach, S. R. H., Brody, G. H., Todorov, A. A., Gunter, T. D., & Philibert, R. A. (2010). Methylation at SLC6A4 is linked to family history of child abuse: An examination of the Iowa Adoptee sample. American Journal of Medical Genetics Part B: Neuropsychiatric Genetics, 153B(2), 710–713. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ajmg.b.31028
effects.ref_replication: Beach, S. R. H., Brody, G. H., Todorov, A. A., Gunter, T. D., & Philibert, R. A. (2011). Methylation at 5HTT Mediates the Impact of Child Sex Abuse on Women’s Antisocial Behavior: An Examination of the Iowa Adoptee Sample. Psychosomatic Medicine, 73(1), 83–87. https://doi.org/10.1097/psy.0b013e3181fdd074
effects.title_original: Methylation at SLC6A4 is linked to family history of child abuse: An examination of the Iowa Adoptee sample
effects.title_replication: Methylation at 5HTT Mediates the Impact of Child Sex Abuse on Women's Antisocial Behavior: An Examination of the Iowa Adoptee Sample
effects.journal_original: American Journal of Medical Genetics Part B: Neuropsychiatric Genetics

effects.description: Infants pointed selectively in the mistaken action con-dition, when the adult had the goal of retrieving the object but an incorrect representation about its location. Importantly, infants provided the new information only when it was relevant to the combination of both the adult’s prior goal representation and her representation of reality.
effects.outcome_quote: We discuss factors that might account for our failure to reproduce the previously reported violation of expectation results on which we were attempting to build. However, we also discuss the consistency of our data with other findings and hypotheses re- garding early-developing theory of mind, and consider the possibility that they reflect the ver- idical abilities of 18-month-old infants, who may track others’ knowledge and ignorance but may not consistently represent the contents of others’ beliefs.
effects.ref_original: Knudsen, B., & Liszkowski, U. (2011). Eighteen‐ and 24‐month‐old infants correct others in anticipation of action mistakes. Developmental Science, 15(1), 113–122. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-7687.2011.01098.x
effects.ref_replication: Powell, L. J., Hobbs, K., Bardis, A., Carey, S., & Saxe, R. (2018). Replications of implicit theory of mind tasks with varying representational demands. Cognitive Development, 46, 40–50. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cogdev.2017.10.004
effects.title_original: Eighteen‐ and 24‐month‐old infants correct others in anticipation of action mistakes
effects.title_replication: Replications of implicit theory of mind tasks with varying representational demands

effects.outcome_quote: These results are consistent with there being at least a small effect in the predicted direction in at least some contexts. We found little evidence for a large effect that is robust to context.

effects.description: Higher scores on the MTQ48 commitment subscale predicted better recall of a second word list after instructions to forget the first list.
effects.outcome_quote: The correlation between mental toughness and directed for- getting replicated. Including a control group with no forget instruction ruled out sustained effort on memory tasks as an explanation; it was specific to directed forgetting. However, mental toughness was shown to correlate with many other personality characteristics, and its effects on directed forgetting were largely due to conscientiousness.
effects.ref_original: Dewhurst, S. A., Anderson, R. J., Cotter, G., Crust, L., & Clough, P. J. (2012). Identifying the cognitive basis of mental toughness: Evidence from the directed forgetting paradigm. Personality and Individual Differences, 53(5), 587–590. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2012.04.036
effects.ref_replication: Delaney, P. F., Goldman, J. A., King, J. S., & Nelson-Gray, R. O. (2015). Mental toughness, reinforcement sensitivity theory, and the five-factor model: Personality and directed forgetting. effects.description: The studies examine whether experiencing near-miss outcomes in scratch card gambling increases the urge to gamble compared to winning or losing outcomes, and whether these outcomes influence further gambling behavior.
effects.outcome_quote: Although near-miss outcomes increased the urge to continue gambling relative to regular losses, no differences in the rates of purchasing were found between the conditions.
effects.ref_original: Stange, M., Graydon, C., & Dixon, M. J. (2016). Increased Urge to Gamble Following Near-Miss Outcomes May Drive Purchasing Behaviour in Scratch Card Gambling. Journal of Gambling Studies, 33(3), 867–879. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10899-016-9662-2
effects.ref_replication: Stange, M., & Dixon, M. J. (2020). Scratch Card Near-Miss Outcomes Increase the Urge to Gamble, but Do Not Impact Further Gambling Behaviour: A Pre-registered Replication and Extension. Journal of Gambling Studies, 36(3), 887–902. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10899-020-09932-7
effects.title_original: Increased Urge to Gamble Following Near-Miss Outcomes May Drive Purchasing Behaviour in Scratch Card Gambling
effects.title_replication: Scratch Card Near-Miss Outcomes Increase the Urge to Gamble, but Do Not Impact Further Gambling Behaviour: A Pre-registered Replication and Extension

effects.description: The current study investigated whether mnemonic demands, in a working memory (WM) task, can drive similar online control modifications.
effects.outcome_quote: Consistent with Jha and Kiyonaga, the present results revealed that mnemonic load led to dynamic adjustments in cognitive control, as reflected by greater performance on trials preceded by high than by low load. In addition, we observed that affective interference could trigger dynamic adjustments in cognitive control, as evinced by higher performance on trials preceded by negative than by neutral distractors.
effects.ref_original: Jha, A. P., & Kiyonaga, A. (2010). Working-memory-triggered dynamic adjustments in cognitive control. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 36(4), 1036–1042. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0019337
effects.ref_replication: Witkin, J. E., Zanesco, A. P., Denkova, E., & Jha, A. P. (2019). Dynamic adjustments in working memory in the face of affective interference. Memory & Cognition, 48(1), 16–31. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13421-019-00958-w
effects.title_original: Working-memory-triggered dynamic adjustments in cognitive control.
effects.title_replication: Dynamic adjustments in working memory in the face of affective interference
effects.journal_original: Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition

effects.description: We primed European Americans and Asian Americans with either a death or a control prime and examined the effect of this manipulation on attitudes about a person who violates cultural norms (Study 1)... Mortality salience promoted culturally divergent responses, leading European Americans to defend the self and Asian Americans to defend other people.
effects.outcome_quote: In the present study, we demonstrated that Japanese individuals who are high on interdependent SC in- deed show greater tolerance toward worldview-threatening other in the MS (vs control) condition, thus replicating the prior research.
effects.ref_original: Ma-Kellams, C., & Blascovich, J. (2011). Culturally Divergent Responses to Mortality Salience. Psychological Science, 22(8), 1019–1024. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611413935
effects.ref_replication: Yanagisawa, K., Kashima, E. S., Moriya, H., Masui, K., Furutani, K., Yoshida, H., Ura, M., & Nomura, M. (2017). Tolerating dissimilar other when primed with death: neural evidence of self-control engaged by interdependent people in Japan. Social Cognitive and Affective Neuroscience, 12(6), 910–917. https://doi.org/10.1093/scan/nsx012
effects.title_original: Culturally Divergent Responses to Mortality Salience
effects.title_replication: Tolerating dissimilar other when primed with death: neural evidence of self-control engaged by interdependent people in Japan

effects.description: Participants required more time and were less accurate discriminating between two letters with lateral mirror-image counterparts (e.g., 'b/d') than between two letters without mirror-image counterparts (e.g., 'a/h').
effects.outcome_quote: the current study aimed at replicating this result in school-aged children. Three age groups—1st, 3rd, and 5th graders— performed a negative priming study in which they were asked to determine on the primes whether two letters were identical and on the probes whether two animals facing opposite directions were identical. All three groups of children required more time to discriminate two letters that were lateral mirror images of one another (e.g., b/d) than two letters that were not (e.g., f/t) [and other results were consistent]
effects.ref_original: Borst, G., Ahr, E., Roell, M., & Houdé, O. (2014). The cost of blocking the mirror generalization process in reading: evidence for the role of inhibitory control in discriminating letters with lateral mirror-image counterparts. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 22(1), 228–234. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-014-0663-9
effects.ref_replication: Ahr, E., Houdé, O., & Borst, G. (2016). Inhibition of the mirror generalization process in reading in school-aged children. Journal of Experimental Child Psychology, 145, 157–165. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jecp.2015.12.009
effects.title_original: The cost of blocking the mirror generalization process in reading: evidence for the role of inhibitory control in discriminating letters with lateral mirror-image counterparts
effects.title_replication: Inhibition of the mirror generalization process in reading in school-aged children

effects.description: People prefer to work together even if individual action is more effective
effects.outcome_quote: Curioni et al.(2022) recently found that participants had a strong preference for complet-ing a virtual box-clearing task cooperatively with a partner rather than alone,despite it being more motorically and cognitively costly. [...] We found a slight preference for individual action over costly joint action, yet this preference was not significantly different from chance.
effects.ref_original: Curioni, A., Voinov, P., Allritz, M., Wolf, T., Call, J., & Knoblich, G. (2022). Human adults prefer to cooperate even when it is costly. Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences, 289(1973). https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2022.0128
effects.ref_replication: Lalli, M., & Carter, M. J. (2023). Human adults prefer to cooperate even when it is costly: A replication of Curioni et al. (2022). OSF Registries. https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/EVDP5

effects.title_replication: Human adults prefer to cooperate even when it is costly: A replication of Curioni et al. (2022)
effects.journal_original: Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences

effects.outcome_quote: In Studies 1, 2, and 4-8, participants indicated greater agreement with, and judged as more likely to be true, statements of the form "A is more than B" than those of the form "B is less than A", despite the same ordinal relation being described in each case.
effects.ref_original: Hoorens, V., & Bruckmüller, S. (2015). Less is more? Think again! A cognitive fluency-based more–less asymmetry in comparative communication. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 109(5), 753–766. https://doi.org/10.1037/pspa0000032

effects.description: JE prediction in the 80-buyer scenario compared to no-buyer scenario
effects.outcome_quote: In Study 1 we replicated the original findings: Relative to people in SE, people in JE overpredicted the happiness derived from quantitatively different hypothetical scenarios (i.e., selling 80 vs 160 books, or 160 vs 240 books), but did not overpredict the difference between qualitatively different hypothetical scenarios (i.e., selling 0 books vs 80 books; 0 being the implicit reference point that makes the two scenarios qualitatively different).
effects.ref_original: Hsee, C. K., & Zhang, J. (2004). Distinction Bias: Misprediction and Mischoice Due to Joint Evaluation. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 86(5), 680–695. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.86.5.680

effects.description: IAT
effects.outcome_quote: In a reanalysis of a previous study and a replication of another study, we found that evidence for the IAT’s advantage over explicit stereotypes in predicting relevant outcomes disappeared when controlling for self-reported liking.
effects.ref_original: Lane, K. A., Goh, J. X., & Driver-Linn, E. (2011). Implicit Science Stereotypes Mediate the Relationship between Gender and Academic Participation. Sex Roles, 66(3–4), 220–234. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11199-011-0036-z

effects.ref_original: Lane, K. A., Goh, J. X., & Driver-Linn, E. (2011). Implicit Science Stereotypes Mediate the Relationship between Gender and Academic Participation. Sex Roles, 66(3–4), 220–234. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11199-011-0036-z
effects.ref_replication: Zitelny, H., Shalom, M., & Bar-Anan, Y. (2017). What Is the Implicit Gender-Science Stereotype? Exploring Correlations Between the Gender-Science IAT and Self-Report Measures. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 8(7), 719–735. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550616683017
effects.title_original: Implicit Science Stereotypes Mediate the Relationship between Gender and Academic Participation
effects.title_replication: What Is the Implicit Gender-Science Stereotype? Exploring Correlations Between the Gender-Science IAT and Self-Report Measures

effects.description: Sacrificing one life to save five using personal force is less morally acceptable than doing so without personal force
effects.outcome_quote: Personal force was associated with decreased ratings of moral acceptability and permissibility, partially replicating experiments 1a and 1b of Greene et al. (2009).
effects.ref_original: Greene, J. D., Cushman, F. A., Stewart, L. E., Lowenberg, K., Nystrom, L. E., & Cohen, J. D. (2009). Pushing moral buttons: The interaction between personal force and intention in moral judgment. Cognition, 111(3), 364–371. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2009.02.001
effects.ref_replication: Lynds, T. M., & Mackinnon, S. P. (2024). Effects of personal force and physical contact on moral decision-making: A replication. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/dca65
effects.title_original: Pushing moral buttons: The interaction between personal force and intention in moral judgment
effects.title_replication: Effects of personal force and physical contact on moral decision-making: A replication

effects.description: More unlikely future events are more abstractly construed. (Trial 1)
effects.outcome_quote: Contrary to the original empirical findings, we present 2 close replication attempts (N 115 and N 120; the original studies had N 20 and N 34) that failed to find the effect of likelihood on construal level. Bayesian analyses provided diagnostic support for the absence of an effect.
effects.ref_original: Wakslak, C. J., Trope, Y., Liberman, N., & Alony, R. (2006). Seeing the forest when entry is unlikely: Probability and the mental representation of events. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 135(4), 641–653. https://doi.org/10.1037/0096-3445.135.4.641
effects.ref_replication: Calderon, S., Mac Giolla, E., Ask, K., & Granhag, P. A. (2020). Subjective likelihood and the construal level of future events: A replication study of Wakslak, Trope, Liberman, and Alony (2006). Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 119(5), e27–e37. https://doi.org/10.1037/pspa0000214
effects.title_original: Seeing the forest when entry is unlikely: Probability and the mental representation of events.
effects.title_replication: Subjective likelihood and the construal level of future events: A replication study of Wakslak, Trope, Liberman, and Alony (2006).
effects.journal_replication: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology

effects.description: Behavioral detection of emotional, high-stakes deception
effects.outcome_quote: Findings do not provide a tidy picture of the reliability of behavioral cues to deception. Although some behavioral cues did replicate across samples, others did not.
effects.ref_original: ten Brinke, L., & Porter, S. (2012). Cry me a river: Identifying the behavioral consequences of extremely high-stakes interpersonal deception. Law and Human Behavior, 36(6), 469–477. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0093929

effects.title_original: Cry me a river: Identifying the behavioral consequences of extremely high-stakes interpersonal deception.
effects.title_replication: Behavioral detection of emotional, high-stakes deception: Replication in a registered report.

effects.description: Fake news game confers psychological resistance against online misinformation

effects.title_replication: Good News about Bad News: Gamified Inoculation Boosts Confidence and Cognitive Immunity Against Fake News

effects.description: numerical anchoring in business name increases price estimations
effects.outcome_quote: No statistically significant evidence of incidental anchoring was obtained. In contrast, robust standard anchoring effects were found. The results suggest that anchoring is limited to situations which require explicit thinking about the anchor.
effects.ref_original: Critcher, C. R., & Gilovich, T. (2007). Incidental environmental anchors. Journal of Behavioral Decision Making, 21(3), 241–251. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/bdm.586
effects.ref_replication: Shanks, D. R., Barbieri-Hermitte, P., & Vadillo, M. A. (2020). Do Incidental Environmental Anchors Bias Consumers’ Price Estimations? Collabra: Psychology, 6(1). https://doi.org/10.1525/collabra.310
effects.title_original: Incidental environmental anchors
effects.title_replication: Do Incidental Environmental Anchors Bias Consumers’ Price Estimations?

effects.description: identifying as victim or perpetrator changes whether one needs power or social acceptance more
effects.outcome_quote: Study 1 supported our hypotheses regarding duals’ emotional needs and behaviors. Replicating and extending previous findings within the framework of the needs-based model, we found that whereas victims and perpetrators showed height- ened need for either agency or positive moral identity, respectively, duals showed enhancement in both needs simul- taneously.
effects.ref_original: Shnabel, N., & Nadler, A. (2008). A needs-based model of reconciliation: Satisfying the differential emotional needs of victim and perpetrator as a key to promoting reconciliation. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 94(1), 116–132. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.116
effects.ref_replication: SimanTov-Nachlieli, I., & Shnabel, N. (2013). Feeling Both Victim and Perpetrator. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 40(3), 301–314. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146167213510746

effects.description: Growing eccentricity negatively influence the emotional categorization (accuracy)
effects.outcome_quote: All our prior findings replicated and eccentricity-based modulation magnitude was smaller with NimStim (~ 16.6% accuracy reduction at 4°) than with KDEF stimuli (~ 27.3% reduction). Our current investigations support our earlier findings that for briefly presented parafoveal stimuli, positive and negative valence perception are differently affected by eccentricity and may be dissociated.
effects.ref_original: Akselevich, V., & Gilaie-Dotan, S. (2022). Positive and negative facial valence perception are modulated differently by eccentricity in the parafovea. Scientific Reports, 12(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24919-7

effects.description: Impact of APOE-ε alleles on brain structure and cognitive function in healthy older adults
effects.outcome_quote: No significant differences were seen across groups, within groups in MRI metrics, or cognitive performance (p>0.05, corrected for multiple comparisons).ConclusionsThe current study partially replicated and extended previous findings from an earlier multimodal study (Honea 2009).

effects.description: Extraversion positively influence the social media time

effects.description: Framing matters: Participants were significantly more likely to consent to the survey when the choice was framed positively (e.g., "Notify me") rather than negatively (e.g., "Do NOT notify me"), regardless of the default setting
effects.outcome_quote: We, however, failed to replicate the findings of Johnson et al. (2002)’s study; we did not find evidence for a default effect
effects.ref_original: Johnson, E. J., Bellman, S., & Lohse, G. L. (2002). Defaults, Framing and Privacy: Why Opting In-Opting Out1. Marketing Letters, 13(1), 5–15. https://doi.org/10.1023/a:1015044207315

effects.description: Defaults matter: Participation rates were significantly higher when the default option was set to "Participate" (requiring the user to Opt-Out to decline) compared to when the default was set to "Not Participate" (requiring the user to Opt-In to accept)
effects.outcome_quote: We, however, failed to replicate the findings of Johnson et al. (2002)’s study; we did not find evidence for a default effect

effects.description: There is no tendency for participants to expect that they would enjoy their future Thanksgiving more than they remembered having enjoyed their past Thanksgiving

effects.description: Liberalism (versus conservatism) was associated with significantly greater conflict-related neural activity between habitual tendency and alternative response (ERN)
effects.outcome_quote: We did not find a relationship between the brain activity connected to conflict monitoring and self-reported conservatism/liberalism orientation (ρ = −0.11, p = 0.297) or conservatism/liberalism validated for the USA agenda (ρ = −0.01, p = 0.910).
effects.ref_original: Amodio, D. M., Jost, J. T., Master, S. L., & Yee, C. M. (2007). Neurocognitive correlates of liberalism and conservatism. Nature Neuroscience, 10(10), 1246–1247. https://doi.org/10.1038/nn1979
effects.ref_replication: Kremláček, J., Musil, D., Langrová, J., & Palecek, M. (2019). Neural Correlates of Liberalism and Conservatism in a Post-communist Country. Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 13. https://doi.org/10.3389/fnhum.2019.00119

effects.description: Self-paced subjects revealed significantly higher performance
effects.outcome_quote: Across four experiments, we found no evidence that control over the onset of stimuli enhances learning. This result stands in notable contrast to the fact that control over stimulus offset does enhance memory (Experiment 1; Tullis & Benjamin, Journal of memory and language, 64 (2), 109-118, 2011).
effects.ref_original: Tullis, J. G., & Benjamin, A. S. (2011). On the effectiveness of self-paced learning. Journal of Memory and Language, 64(2), 109–118. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jml.2010.11.002
effects.ref_replication: Patel, T. N., Steyvers, M., & Benjamin, A. S. (2019). Monitoring the ebb and flow of attention: Does controlling the onset of stimuli during encoding enhance memory? Memory & Cognition, 47(4), 706–718. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13421-019-00899-4
effects.title_original: On the effectiveness of self-paced learning
effects.title_replication: Monitoring the ebb and flow of attention: Does controlling the onset of stimuli during encoding enhance memory?

effects.description: Performance on the item level was greater for self-paced subjects
effects.outcome_quote: Across four experiments, we found no evidence that control over the onset of stimuli enhances learning. This result stands in notable contrast to the fact that control over stimulus offset does enhance memory (Experiment 1; Tullis & Benjamin, Journal of memory and language, 64 (2), 109-118, 2011).

effects.description: Altruism is negatively associated with the political orientation
effects.outcome_quote: aiming to replicate this link, we assumed that altruism also influences hypothetical and actual voting behavior as well as endorsement of specific, currently debated political agendas. effects.description: Compared to working alone with no encouraging statements, participants exercise longer when working together under conjunctive task demands with a moderately superior virtual partner, whether that partner provides encouraging statements that use the exclusive “you” pronoun or no encouragement at all
effects.outcome_quote: Newly collected data corroborated Irwin et al.’s (2013) finding that encouragement using the ambiguous and exclusive pronoun “you” (e.g. “you can do it”) from a virtually present partner significantly weakened this Köhler effect. Some partial support was found for Irwin et al.’s expla- nation of this effect. Irwin et al. suggested that when one hears one’s partner say things like “you can do it!”, that may be interpreted that the partner is him/herself struggling and is to some degree trying to encourage him/herself. Hence, that partner may not be as superior as previously thought.
effects.ref_original: Irwin, B. C., Feltz, D. L., & Kerr, N. L. (2013). Silence is Golden: Effect of Encouragement in Motivating the Weak Link in an Online Exercise Video Game. Journal of Medical Internet Research, 15(6), e104. https://doi.org/10.2196/jmir.2551
effects.ref_replication: Max, E. J., Feltz, D. L., Kerr, N. L., & Wittenbaum, G. M. (2016). Is silence really golden? Effect of encouragement from a partner or trainer on active video game play. International Journal of Sport and Exercise Psychology, 16(3), 261–275. https://doi.org/10.1080/1612197x.2016.1199580
effects.title_original: Silence is Golden: Effect of Encouragement in Motivating the Weak Link in an Online Exercise Video Game
effects.title_replication: Is silence really golden? Effect of encouragement from a partner or trainer on active video game play
effects.journal_replication: International Journal of Sport and Exercise Psychology

effects.description: Recalling unethical deeds darkens perceived room brightness
effects.outcome_quote: We replicated the methods of these two original studies with four high-powered replication studies (two online and two in the laboratory). Our results did not differ significantly from zero, 9 out of 10 of the effects were significantly smaller than the originally reported effects, and the effects were not consistently moderated by individual difference measures of potential discrepancies between the original and the replication samples.
effects.ref_original: Banerjee, P., Chatterjee, P., & Sinha, J. (2012). Is It Light or Dark? Recalling Moral Behavior Changes Perception of Brightness. Psychological Science, 23(4), 407–409. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611432497
effects.ref_replication: Brandt, M. J., IJzerman, H., & Blanken, I. (2014). Does Recalling Moral Behavior Change the Perception of Brightness? Social Psychology, 45(3), 246–252. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000191

effects.description: People lacking social warmth compensate through physical warmth in the form of hot showers/baths
effects.outcome_quote: Study 1 was a near-exact replication of Study 1a reported in Bargh and Shalev (2012) using a sample size that was more than four times larger than the original (n = 51 college students; 26 women; M age = 20.11 years, SD = 4.17). In contrast to the original results, we found a trivial correlation in the opposite direction of the correlation reported in Bargh and Shalev. Thus, we did not duplicate their original result with college students using either the significance test criterion or the effect size criterion.
effects.ref_original: Bargh, J. A., & Shalev, I. (2012). The substitutability of physical and social warmth in daily life. Emotion, 12(1), 154–162. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0023527

effects.description: participants required more time when determining that two animals (but not two buildings) were mirror images of each other when preceded by letters with mirror-image counterparts than with-out mirror-image counterparts (‘a/h’).
effects.outcome_quote: the current study aimed at replicating this result in school-aged children. Three age groups—1st, 3rd, and 5th graders— performed a negative priming study in which they were asked to determine on the primes whether two letters were identical and on the probes whether two animals facing opposite directions were identical. All three groups of children required more time to discriminate two letters that were lateral mirror images of one another (e.g., b/d) than two letters that were not (e.g., f/t) [and other results were consistent]
effects.ref_original: Borst, G., Ahr, E., Roell, M., & Houdé, O. (2014). The cost of blocking the mirror generalization process in reading: evidence for the role of inhibitory control in discriminating letters with lateral mirror-image counterparts. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 22(1), 228–234. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-014-0663-9
effects.ref_replication: Ahr, E., Houdé, O., & Borst, G. (2016). Inhibition of the mirror generalization process in reading in school-aged children. Journal of Experimental Child Psychology, 145, 157–165. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jecp.2015.12.009
effects.title_original: The cost of blocking the mirror generalization process in reading: evidence for the role of inhibitory control in discriminating letters with lateral mirror-image counterparts
effects.title_replication: Inhibition of the mirror generalization process in reading in school-aged children

Three age groups—1st, 3rd, and 5th graders— performed a negative priming study in which they were asked to determine on the primes whether two letters were identical and on the probes whether two animals facing opposite directions were identical. All three groups of children required more time to discriminate two letters that were lateral mirror images of one another (e.g., b/d) than two letters that were not (e.g., f/t) [and other results were consistent]
effects.ref_original: Borst, G., Ahr, E., Roell, M., & Houdé, O. (2014). The cost of blocking the mirror generalization process in reading: evidence for the role of inhibitory control in discriminating letters with lateral mirror-image counterparts. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 22(1), 228–234. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-014-0663-9
effects.ref_replication: Ahr, E., Houdé, O., & Borst, G. (2016). Inhibition of the mirror generalization process in reading in school-aged children. Journal of Experimental Child Psychology, 145, 157–165. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jecp.2015.12.009
effects.title_original: The cost of blocking the mirror generalization process in reading: evidence for the role of inhibitory control in discriminating letters with lateral mirror-image counterparts
effects.title_replication: Inhibition of the mirror generalization process in reading in school-aged children

effects.description: participants required more time when determining that two animals (but not two buildings) were mirror images of each other when preceded by letters with mirror-image counterparts than with-out mirror-image counterparts (‘a/h’).

effects.description: Unemployment’s influence on FFM personality traits (Openness)
effects.outcome_quote: Latent change analyses indicated no effect of job loss on any Big Five trait. Moderating effects of unemployment duration or gender were not found. Even analyses accounting for potential selection effects led to comparable results. Thus, personality seemed invariant despite changes in employment status.
effects.ref_original: Boyce, C. J., Wood, A. M., Daly, M., & Sedikides, C. (2015). Personality change following unemployment. Journal of Applied Psychology, 100(4), 991–1011. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0038647
effects.ref_replication: Gnambs, T., & Stiglbauer, B. (2019). No personality change following unemployment: A registered replication of Boyce, Wood, Daly, and Sedikides (2015). https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/3bhja
effects.title_original: Personality change following unemployment.
effects.title_replication: No personality change following unemployment: A registered replication of Boyce, Wood, Daly, and Sedikides (2015)

effects.description: Group therapy participants had later "not on track" alerts than individual therapy participants
effects.outcome_quote: he current study replicated the significant difference found in the rate of NOT alerts between treatment formats (p ! .007). Additionally, the timing of NOT alerts created a more complex picture. Burlingame et al.’s (2018) results may be more common as preliminary results suggest that clients in group therapy are more likely to alert as NOT during the course of therapy when compared to clients in individual therapy.
effects.ref_original: Burlingame, G. M., Whitcomb, K. E., Woodland, S. C., Olsen, J. A., Beecher, M., & Gleave, R. (2018). The effects of relationship and progress feedback in group psychotherapy using the Group Questionnaire and Outcome Questionnaire–45: A randomized clinical trial. Psychotherapy, 55(2), 116–131. https://doi.org/10.1037/pst0000133
effects.ref_replication: Alldredge, C., Burlingame, G., Olsen, J., & Van Epps, J. (2020). Outcome Questionnaire-45 (OQ-45) progress alert rates in group versus individual treatment: An archival replication. Group Dynamics: Theory, Research, and Practice, 24(4), 247–260. https://doi.org/10.1037/gdn0000121
effects.title_original: The effects of relationship and progress feedback in group psychotherapy using the Group Questionnaire and Outcome Questionnaire–45: A randomized clinical trial.
effects.title_replication: Outcome Questionnaire-45 (OQ-45) progress alert rates in group versus individual treatment: An archival replication.
effects.journal_replication: Group Dynamics: Theory, Research, and Practice

effects.description: Power posing decreases cortisol levels
effects.outcome_quote: Consistent with the findings of Carney et al., our results showed a significant effect of power posing on self-reported feelings of power. However, we found no significant effect of power posing on hormonal levels or in any of the three behavioral tasks.
effects.ref_original: Carney, D. R., Cuddy, A. J. C., & Yap, A. J. (2010). Power Posing. Psychological Science, 21(10), 1363–1368. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797610383437

effects.description: with treatment, the early intensive behavioral treatment group obtained significantly higher IQ ... than did the comparison group
effects.outcome_quote: Linear mixed models indicated that EIBI participants improved significantly more than SAU participants on standardized tests of IQ, nonverbal IQ, adaptive behavior, and academic achievement, administered by independent evaluators. [...] the study provides evidence for the sustainability of effective EIBI in community settings for children with ASD who start intervention at varying ages throughout early childhood.

effects.description: Anchor values that are incidentally present in the environment can affect a person’s numerical estimates, Item: Restaurant
effects.outcome_quote: No statistically significant evidence of incidental anchoring was obtained. In contrast, robust standard anchoring effects were found. The results suggest that anchoring is limited to situations which require explicit thinking about the anchor.

effects.description: Anchor values that are incidentally present in the environment can affect a person’s numerical estimates, Item: Restaurant
effects.outcome_quote: No statistically significant evidence of incidental anchoring was obtained. In contrast, robust standard anchoring effects were found. The results suggest that anchoring is limited to situations which require explicit thinking about the anchor.
effects.ref_original: Critcher, C. R., & Gilovich, T. (2007). Incidental environmental anchors. Journal of Behavioral Decision Making, 21(3), 241–251. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/bdm.586
effects.ref_replication: Shanks, D. R., Barbieri-Hermitte, P., & Vadillo, M. A. (2020). Do Incidental Environmental Anchors Bias Consumers’ Price Estimations? Collabra: Psychology, 6(1). https://doi.org/10.1525/collabra.310

effects.description: People perform better when playing with a "lucky" ball
effects.outcome_quote: We conducted two high-powered and precise replications [...] We found that the effect of superstition on performance is elusive: Participants told they had a lucky ball performed almost identically to controls. Our failure to replicate the target study was not due to lack of impact, lack of statistical power, differences in task difficulty, nor differences in participant belief in luck.
effects.ref_original: Damisch, L., Stoberock, B., & Mussweiler, T. (2010). Keep Your Fingers Crossed! Psychological Science, 21(7), 1014–1020. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797610372631
effects.ref_replication: Calin-Jageman, R. J., & Caldwell, T. L. (2014). Replication of the Superstition and Performance Study by. Social Psychology, 45(3), 239–245. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000190

effects.description: Consumers view larger products as higher status.
effects.outcome_quote: We conducted a close replication of this finding (N = 415), and observed a nonsignificant effect in the opposite direction [...]. We discuss potential reasons for this unsuccessful replication as well as implications for the status-signaling literature in consumer psychology.
effects.ref_original: Dubois, D., Rucker, D. D., & Galinsky, A. D. (2011). Retracted: Super Size Me: Product Size as a Signal of Status. Journal of Consumer Research, 38(6), 1047–1062. https://doi.org/10.1086/661890
effects.ref_replication: Tunca, B., Ziano, I., & Wenting, X. (2022). Super-Size Me: An Unsuccessful Preregistered Replication of the Effect of Product Size on Status Signaling. Meta-Psychology, 6. https://doi.org/10.15626/mp.2020.2538

effects.description: Airbnb hosts who look more trustworthy in their profile photos charge higher nightly prices and are chosen more often by guests.
effects.outcome_quote: Ert, Fleischer, and Magen (2016) found that more trustworthy-looking, but not more attractive-looking, Airbnb hosts from Stockholm charge higher prices for similar apartments. [...] Our results support the general conclusion that people rely on profile photos in online markets, though we find that attractiveness is more important than trust- worthiness.
effects.ref_original: Ert, E., Fleischer, A., & Magen, N. (2016). Trust and reputation in the sharing economy: The role of personal photos in Airbnb. Tourism Management, 55, 62–73. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tourman.2016.01.013
effects.ref_replication: Jaeger, B., Sleegers, W. W. A., Evans, A. M., Stel, M., & van Beest, I. (2019). The effects of facial attractiveness and trustworthiness in online peer-to-peer markets. Journal of Economic Psychology, 75, 102125. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joep.2018.11.004
effects.title_original: Trust and reputation in the sharing economy: The role of personal photos in Airbnb
effects.title_replication: The effects of facial attractiveness and trustworthiness in online peer-to-peer markets

effects.description: Gustatory disgust triggers a heightened sense of moral wrongness: Bitter vs. Control
effects.outcome_quote: Some were in the opposite of the predicted direction; all had 95% confidence intervals containing zero, and all were smaller than the effect size the original authors could have meaningfully detected. [...] Bayes factor tests revealed greater relative support for the null than for the replication hypothesis. The overall pattern provides little to no support for the theory that physical disgust via taste perception harshens judgments of moral wrongness.
effects.ref_original: Eskine, K. J., Kacinik, N. A., & Prinz, J. J. (2011). A Bad Taste in the Mouth. Psychological Science, 22(3), 295–299. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611398497

effects.description: Concretely worded statements are judged to be more likely to be true than abstract equivalents
effects.outcome_quote: In Experiment 1a, the effect of concreteness on judgements of truth (Cohen’sdz = 0.08; 95% CI: [–0.03, 0.18]) was smaller than that of the original study. [...] Collectively, the pattern of results is inconsistent with that of the original study.
effects.ref_original: Hansen, J., & Wänke, M. (2010). Truth From Language and Truth From Fit: The Impact of Linguistic Concreteness and Level of Construal on Subjective Truth. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 36(11), 1576–1588. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146167210386238

effects.description: Prenatal exposure to the synthetic glucocorticoid dexamethasone (or to chronic mild stress) leads to a heightened acoustic startle response (ASR) in adult rat offspring.
effects.outcome_quote: We hereby replicated our earlier findings in a new strain and laboratory, thus strengthening the validity of our model regarding prenatal stress effects on ASR in female offspring.
effects.ref_original: Hougaard, K. S., Andersen, M. B., Kjær, S. L., Hansen, Å. M., Werge, T., & Lund, S. P. (2005). Prenatal stress may increase vulnerability to life events: Comparison with the effects of prenatal dexamethasone. Developmental Brain Research, 159(1), 55–63. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.devbrainres.2005.06.014
effects.ref_replication: Kjær, S. L., Hougaard, K. S., Tasker, R. A., MacDonald, D. S., Rosenberg, R., Elfving, B., & Wegener, G. (2011). Influence of diurnal phase on startle response in adult rats exposed to dexamethasone in utero. Physiology & Behavior, 102(5), 444–452. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physbeh.2010.12.015
effects.title_original: Prenatal stress may increase vulnerability to life events: Comparison with the effects of prenatal dexamethasone
effects.title_replication: Influence of diurnal phase on startle response in adult rats exposed to dexamethasone in utero

effects.description: Participants receiving the $45 scarf will consider their friend more generous than those receiving the $55 coat.
effects.outcome_quote: We found support for “less is better” across all studies (N = 403; Study 1 original d = 0.70 [0.24,1.15], replication d = 0.99 [0.72,1.26]; Study 2 original d = 0.74 [0.12,1.35], replication d = 0.32 [0.07,0.56]; Study 4 original d = 0.97 [0.43,1.50], replication d = 0.76 [0.50,1.02]), with weaker support for “more is better”.
effects.ref_original: Hsee, C. K. (1998). Less is better: when low-value options are valued more highly than high-value options. Journal of Behavioral Decision Making, 11(2), 107–121. https://doi.org/10.1002/(sici)1099-0771(199806)11:23.0.co;2-y

effects.description: Music book is relatively affect-rich and that the cash
effects.outcome_quote: Replicating their Study 2, we found support for the hypothesis that receiving affect-rich music books was less affected by monetary value compared to affect-poor situation of receiving mere cash (original: η 2 = 0.02, 90% CI [0.003, 0.05]; replication: η2 = 0.01, 90% CI [0.003, 0.03]).
effects.ref_original: Hsee, C. K., & Rottenstreich, Y. (2004). Music, Pandas, and Muggers: On the Affective Psychology of Value. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 133(1), 23–30. https://doi.org/10.1037/0096-3445.133.1.23

effects.description: Consumers will be more likely to use a holistic process (vs. an attribute-by-attribute comparison) to judge anthropomorphized products.
effects.outcome_quote: Indeed, perhaps the most noteworthy finding is that the two replications produced extremely similar results, despite coming from different online populations. One conclusion could be that the original effect does not truly exist, and the original finding was a false positive. Our evidence is consistent with that.
effects.ref_original: Huang, F., Wong, V. C., & Wan, E. W. (2019). The Influence of Product Anthropomorphism on Comparative Judgment. Journal of Consumer Research. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucz028
effects.ref_replication: Simmons, J. &. Nelson, L.D. (2020). [87] Data Replicada #5: Do Human-Like Products Inspire More Holistic Judgments? Data Colada.
effects.title_original: The Influence of Product Anthropomorphism on Comparative Judgment

effects.description: Reading literary fiction can led to better performance on tests of affective theory of mind compared to reading popular fiction
effects.outcome_quote: The current non-replication of the results casts a further shadow on the conclusions of the Science article... Even when using the exact same selected text samples, methods, and population as Kidd and Castano, one does not necessarily obtain the same results. ... Strong claims such as ‘reading literature makes us better people’ require strong evidence. And it is now quite clear that such strong evidence is completely absent from the research of Kidd and Castano.
effects.ref_original: Kidd, D. C., & Castano, E. (2013). Reading Literary Fiction Improves Theory of Mind. Science, 342(6156), 377–380. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1239918
effects.ref_replication: Dijkstra, K., Verkoeijen, P., van Kuijk, I., Yee Chow, S., Bakker, A., & Zwaan, R. (2015). Leidt het lezen van literaire fictie tot meer empathie? : een replicatiestudie van Kidd en Castano (2013). De Psycholoog, 50(10), 10–21. Retrieved from http://hdl.handle.net/1765/98670
effects.title_original: Reading Literary Fiction Improves Theory of Mind
effects.title_replication: Leidt het lezen van literaire fictie tot meer empathie? : een replicatiestudie van Kidd en Castano (2013)

effects.description: effects of a voluntary summer reading intervention with teacher and parent scaffolding on measures of silent reading ability (Iowa Test of Basic Skills [ITBS]).
effects.outcome_quote: Overall, there were no significant treatment effects [..] However, there was a significant interaction between the treatment conditions and poverty measured at the school level. [...] The results underscore the impor- tance of looking at patterns of treatment effects across different contexts, settings, and populations.
effects.ref_original: Kim, J. S., & White, T. G. (2008). Scaffolding Voluntary Summer Reading for Children in Grades 3 to 5: An Experimental Study. Scientific Studies of Reading, 12(1), 1–23. https://doi.org/10.1080/10888430701746849

effects.title_original: Scaffolding Voluntary Summer Reading for Children in Grades 3 to 5: An Experimental Study
effects.title_replication: Replicating the Effects of a Teacher-Scaffolded Voluntary Summer Reading Program: The Role of Poverty

effects.description: Willingness to contribute for an identified single victim is greater than the willingness to contribute for an unidentified single victim, with a weaker effect for a group of victims (singularity and identifiability interaction)
effects.outcome_quote: The replication findings failed to provide empirical support for the identified single victim effect hypothesis since we found no evidence of differences in willingness to contribute when comparing a single identified victim to a single unidentified victim [...] and no indication for the target article’s interaction between singularity and identifiability

effects.description: Moral Priming
effects.outcome_quote: In the primary meta-analysis (19 replications, total n = 4,674), participants who were given an opportunity to cheat reported solving 0.11 more matrices if they were given a moral reminder than if they were given a neutral reminder (95% confidence interval = [−0.09, 0.31]). This small effect was numerically in the opposite direction of the effect observed in the original study (Cohen’s d = −0.04).
effects.ref_original: Mazar, N., Amir, O., & Ariely, D. (2008). The Dishonesty of Honest People: A Theory of Self-Concept Maintenance. Journal of Marketing Research, 45(6), 633–644. https://doi.org/10.1509/jmkr.45.6.633
effects.ref_replication: Verschuere, B., Meijer, E. H., Jim, A., Hoogesteyn, K., Orthey, R., McCarthy, R. J., Skowronski, J. J., Acar, O. A., Aczel, B., Bakos, B. E., Barbosa, F., Baskin, E., Bègue, L., Ben-Shakhar, G., Birt, A. R., Blatz, L., Charman, S. D., Claesen, A., Clay, S. L., … Yıldız, E. (2018). Registered Replication Report on Mazar, Amir, and Ariely (2008). Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science, 1(3), 299–317. https://doi.org/10.1177/2515245918781032
effects.title_original: The Dishonesty of Honest People: A Theory of Self-Concept Maintenance
effects.title_replication: Registered Replication Report on Mazar, Amir, and Ariely (2008)
effects.journal_replication: Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science

effects.description: Repeated checking (or even repeated non-checking exposure) of a virtual stove progressively reduces memory confidence, vividness, detail and accuracy relative to an irrelevant control stimulus (light bulbs).
effects.outcome_quote: we consistently replicated the increase in memory distrust across repeated trials that is typically found with the stove-checking task
effects.ref_original: Medway, M., & Jones, M. K. (2013). Further Evidence That Repeated Checking Leads to Reduced Memory Confidence, Vividness and Detail: New Evidence That Repeated Object Exposure Also Results in Memory Distrust. Behaviour Change, 30(3), 159–179. https://doi.org/10.1017/bec.2013.16
effects.ref_replication: Burns, D. J., Dalterio, C. H., Burns, S. A., & Coelho, G. V. (2020). Not all checking decreases memory confidence: Implications for obsessive-compulsive disorder. Journal of Behavior Therapy and Experimental Psychiatry, 69, 101573. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbtep.2020.101573
effects.title_original: Further Evidence That Repeated Checking Leads to Reduced Memory Confidence, Vividness and Detail: New Evidence That Repeated Object Exposure Also Results in Memory Distrust

effects.description: The frequency estimate for the “and” conjunction phrase will be higher than the phrase describing unlikely target alone.
effects.outcome_quote: Linda profile findings were in sup- port of conjunction effect and consistent with Tversky and Kahneman’s (1983) arguments for a representative heuristic. effects.description: women shy away from competition and men embrace it
effects.outcome_quote: Our results are in line with the results by Niederle and Vesterlund (2007), who found that twice as many men as women chose the competitive alternative.
effects.ref_original: Niederle, M., & Vesterlund, L. (2007). Do Women Shy Away From Competition? Do Men Compete Too Much? The Quarterly Journal of Economics, 122(3), 1067–1101. https://doi.org/10.1162/qjec.122.3.1067
effects.ref_replication: Carlsson, F., Lampi, E., Martinsson, P., & Yang, X. (2020). Replication: Do women shy away from competition? Experimental evidence from China. Journal of Economic Psychology, 81, 102312. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joep.2020.102312

effects.description: Senior police officers' tactical gaze control and visual attention improve with an individual videobased police firearms training
effects.outcome_quote: Overall, the previous findings were replicated: Baseline levels of performance were elevated, yet the intervention group significantly improved their response time and time until the first hit.

effects.description: Input, Process, and Output Anxiety Scales predict general language learning anxiety
effects.outcome_quote: In both the initial as well as the replication study, evidence showed that language learners experience significantly higher levels of output anxiety than input or process anxiety; nonetheless, these three types of anxiety were found to be linked to the general concept of language anxiety to different degrees, with process anxiety depicting the strongest association according to the results of data analysis in the initial and the replication study. Although the scales could be characterized as reliable, their componential struc- ture was found to be not without flaws. Some of the same items of the scales were labelled as problematic in the Hungarian version of the scales as well as in the English version. As such, it can be said that these issues are inherent to the items rather than to their translated versions and thus warrant reconsideration.
effects.ref_original: Onwuegbuzie, A. J., Bailey, P., & Daley, C. E. (2000). The Validation of Three Scales Measuring Anxiety at Different Stages of the Foreign Language Learning Process: The Input Anxiety Scale, the Processing Anxiety Scale, and the Output Anxiety Scale. Language Learning, 50(1), 87–117. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/0023-8333.00112
effects.ref_replication: Piniel, K. (2024). The Input, Process, and Output Anxiety Scales: An Approximate Replication Study. Investigating Foreign Language Anxiety, 49–71. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-55844-3_4
effects.title_original: The Validation of Three Scales Measuring Anxiety at Different Stages of the Foreign Language Learning Process: The Input Anxiety Scale, the Processing Anxiety Scale, and the Output Anxiety Scale
effects.title_replication: The Input, Process, and Output Anxiety Scales: An Approximate Replication Study
effects.journal_replication: Second Language Learning and Teaching,Investigating Foreign Language Anxiety

effects.outcome_quote: So then we gave up, and decided to call the whole thing “inconclusive.”
effects.ref_original: Park, H., Lalwani, A. K., & Silvera, D. H. (2019). The Impact of Resource Scarcity on Price-Quality Judgments. Journal of Consumer Research, 46(6), 1110–1124. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucz031
effects.ref_replication: Simmons, J. &. Nelson, L.D. (2020). [89] Data Replicada #6: The Problem of (Weird) Differential Attrition. Data Colada.

effects.description: Giving people an 'accuracy nudge' before showing them headlines make them more discerning about their accuracy and less likely to share false articles on social media
effects.outcome_quote: The first stage of the replication test ( n = 701) was unsuccessful ( p = .67). After collecting a second round of data (additional n = 882, pooled N = 1,583), we found a small but significant interaction between treatment condition and truth discernment (uncorrected p = .017; treatment: d = 0.14, control: d = 0.10). As in the target study, perceived headline accuracy correlated with treatment impact, so that treatment-group participants were less willing to share headlines that were perceived as less accurate.

effects.description: Having variety leads people to pour more candy, presumably because variety lowers the perceived quantity.
effects.outcome_quote: We conducted six studies with methods that were similar to those used by Redden and Hoch and did not find this effect in any of them. A meta-analysis of the four original studies and 6 replication studies (N = 1,383) revealed no evidence for the phenomenon that variety reduces perceived quantity.
effects.ref_original: Redden, J. P., & Hoch, S. J. (2009). The Presence of Variety Reduces Perceived Quantity. Journal of Consumer Research, 36(3), 406–417. https://doi.org/10.1086/598971

effects.description: People are typically poor at telling apart truthful and deceptive statements. Based on the Unconscious Thought Theory, it has been suggested that poor lie detection arises from the intrinsic limitations of conscious thinking and can be improved by facilitating the contribution of unconscious thought (UT).
effects.outcome_quote: Results from two experiments (combined N = 226) failed to reveal a significant difference in lie detection accuracy between the thinking modes, even after efforts were made to facilitate the occurrence of an UT advantage in Experiment 2. The results imply that the UT advantage in deception detection is not a robust phenomenon.
effects.ref_original: Reinhard, M.-A., Greifeneder, R., & Scharmach, M. (2013). Unconscious processes improve lie detection. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 105(5), 721–739. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0034352
effects.ref_replication: Moi, W. Y., & Shanks, D. R. (2015). Can lies be detected unconsciously? Frontiers in Psychology, 6. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2015.01221
effects.title_original: Unconscious processes improve lie detection.
effects.title_replication: Can lies be detected unconsciously?
effects.journal_original: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology

effects.description: participants were willing to pay a small fee to postpone a decision about a vacation package promotion when outcome of an exam was uncertain, despite preferences to purchase the package regardless of exam outcome.
effects.outcome_quote: These results were consistent with the original findings for the “paying to know“ problem (original: Cramer’s V = 0.22, 95% (CI) [0.14, 0.32]; replication: Cramer’s V = 0.30, 95% CI [0.24, 0.37]), yet not for the “choice under risk” problem (original: Cramer’s V = 0.26, 95% CI [0.14, 0.39]; replication: Cramer’s V = 0.11, 95% CI [− 0.07, 0.20]).
effects.ref_original: Tversky, A., & Shafir, E. (1992). The Disjunction Effect in Choice under Uncertainty. Psychological Science, 3(5), 305–310. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.1992.tb00678.x
effects.ref_replication: Ziano, I., Kong, M. F., Kim, H. J., Liu, C. Y., Wong, S. C., Cheng, B. L., & Feldman, G. (2021). Replication: Revisiting Tversky and Shafir’s (1992) Disjunction Effect with an extension comparing between and within subject designs. Journal of Economic Psychology, 83, 102350. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joep.2020.102350
effects.title_original: The Disjunction Effect in Choice under Uncertainty
effects.title_replication: Replication: Revisiting Tversky and Shafir’s (1992) Disjunction Effect with an extension comparing between and within subject designs

effects.description: forgoing an initial attractive opportunity decreases the likelihood of taking a subsequent opportunity that is less attractive, even when the subsequent opportunity still offers positive value
effects.outcome_quote: We found consistent findings across samples, with the inaction inertia effect dependent on the scenario used.
effects.ref_original: Tykocinski, O. E., Pittman, T. S., & Tuttle, E. E. (1995). Inaction inertia: Foregoing future benefits as a result of an initial failure to act. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 68(5), 793–803. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.68.5.793
effects.ref_replication: Chen, J., Hui, L. S., Yu, T., Feldman, G., Zeng, S., Ching, T. L., Ng, C. H., Wu, K. W., Yuen, C. M., Lau, T. K., Cheng, B. L., & Ng, K. W. (2020). Foregone Opportunities and Choosing Not to Act: Replications of Inaction Inertia Effect. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 12(3), 333–345. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550619900570
effects.title_original: Inaction inertia: Foregoing future benefits as a result of an initial failure to act.
effects.title_replication: Foregone Opportunities and Choosing Not to Act: Replications of Inaction Inertia Effect
effects.journal_original: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology

effects.description: Lower belief in free will leads to cheating
effects.outcome_quote: We found that the revisions did not matter. Although the revised measure of belief in free will had better reliability than the original measure, an analysis of the data from the two protocols combined indicated that free-will beliefs were unchanged by the manipulations, d = 0.064, 95% confidence interval = [−0.087, 0.22], and in the focal test, there were no differences in cheating behavior between conditions, d = 0.076, 95% CI = [−0.082, 0.22].
effects.ref_original: Vohs, K. D., & Schooler, J. W. (2008). The Value of Believing in Free Will. Psychological Science, 19(1), 49–54. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02045.x
effects.ref_replication: Buttrick, N. R., Aczel, B., Aeschbach, L. F., Bakos, B. E., Brühlmann, F., Claypool, H. M., Hüffmeier, J., Kovacs, M., Schuepfer, K., Szecsi, P., Szuts, A., Szöke, O., Thomae, M., Torka, A.-K., Walker, R. J., & Wood, M. J. (2020). effects.description: Analytic Priming
effects.outcome_quote: We observed little to no effect of the experimental manipulation on religious belief (d = 0.07 in the wrong direction, 95% CI[-0.12, 0.25], N = 941). The original finding does not seem to provide reli- able or valid evidence that analytic thinking causes a decrease in religious belief.
effects.ref_original: Gervais, W. M., & Norenzayan, A. (2012). Analytic Thinking Promotes Religious Disbelief. Science, 336(6080), 493–496. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1215647
effects.ref_replication: Sanchez, C., Sundermeier, B., Gray, K., & Calin-Jageman, R. J. (2017). Direct replication of Gervais & Norenzayan (2012): No evidence that analytic thinking decreases religious belief. PLOS ONE, 12(2), e0172636. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0172636
effects.title_original: Analytic Thinking Promotes Religious Disbelief
effects.title_replication: Direct replication of Gervais & Norenzayan (2012): No evidence that analytic thinking decreases religious belief

effects.description: When partial correlations were computed controlling for the overlap between PMS [personal moral standards] and CMM [concern over moral mistakes], PMS showed positive correlations with moral values, virtues, reciprocal helping, forgiveness... In contrast, CMM showed a positive correlation only with indebtedness...
effects.outcome_quote: The present study aimed to replicate Yang et al.'s findings in a sample of Western students (N = 243) [...] Results largely replicated Yang et al.'s findings.
effects.ref_original: Yang, H., Stoeber, J., & Wang, Y. (2015). Moral perfectionism and moral values, virtues, and judgments: A preliminary investigation. Personality and Individual Differences, 75, 229–233. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2014.11.040
effects.ref_replication: Stoeber, J., & Yang, H. (2016). Moral perfectionism and moral values, virtues, and judgments: Further investigations. Personality and Individual Differences, 88, 6–11. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2015.08.031

effects.description: Scarcity leads to greater focus.
effects.outcome_quote: In the subsequent replications, we find consistent and strong evidence for the motivating hypothesis of the original paper: Scarcity itself leads to over-borrowing. We see this clearly in Experiments 2, 3, and 4 where poor participants performed significantly worse when they had the flexibility to borrow. [...] But we did not find a greater focus on the current round (estimated via the amount of time spent aiming shots) to be associated with higher borrowing rates.
effects.ref_original: Shah, A. K., Mullainathan, S., & Shafir, E. (2012). Some Consequences of Having Too Little. Science, 338(6107), 682–685. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1222426
